sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 2 whatthisdissertationterms:acompoundedvulnerabilityinwhichthe doublethermaljeopardy samehouseholdsfaceinadequatewarmthinwinteranddangerousoverheatinginsummer,arising fromthesamestructuralconstraints. Theurgencyofaddressingdoublethermaljeopardyliesnotonlyinmeetingcarbontargetsbut alsoinconfrontingstructuralsocialinequality.Fuelpoverty,theincapacitytoaffordadequate heatingandcoolingtomaintainasafeandcomfortablehome,disproportionatelyaffectslow- incomehouseholds,singleparents,olderadults,andindividualsfromdiverseethnicbackgrounds (Gillardetal.,2017).Enduringfuelpovertydetrimentallyaffectshealth,mentalwell-being,and educationaloutcomes,andisassociatedwithprematuremortality,withhundredsofthousands oflivesaffectedbycoldhomesacrossEuropeeachwinter.Householdsexperiencingfuelpoverty typicallylackthefinancialmeansortenuresecuritytoretrofittheirhomeseffectively,trappingthem inconditionsofwinterunder-heatingwhilesimultaneouslyincreasingtheirexposuretosummer heatasinsulationandairtightnessimprovementsaremadewithoutcorrespondingventilation upgrades.Ratherthantreatingwintercoldstressandsummeroverheatingasindependentproblems, thisresearchconceptualisesthehouseholdsmostaffectedbybothas:groups thermalminorities whoseeverydaythermalexperiencessystematicallyfalloutsidenormativeassumptionsofcomfort andsafety.Forthesehouseholds,structuralinequalityisnotseasonalbutyear-round,embeddedin thephysicalfabricoftheirdwellingsandamplifiedbyanincreasinglyvolatileclimate. Despitetheimportanceofunderstandingthisdualvulnerability,currentresearchremains fragmented.Thestudyofhard-to-decarbonisehousinghashistoricallyreceivedlimitedscholarly attention,andbasicmethodsforidentifyingandcharacterisingHtDtypologiesatscalearestill beingestablished(RaslanandAmbrose,2022).Morefundamentally,fuelpovertyandwinter coldresearchoperatelargelyindependentlyofoverheatingandsummerheatstressliterature, leavingthehouseholdswhoexperiencebothformsofthermalriskanalyticallyinvisible.This seasonalseparationalsoproducesmethodologicalblindspots:energyefficiencyratingsdesignedto captureheat-lossperformancedonotaddressoverheatingrisk,andoverheatingassessmentsrarely engagewithretrofitbarriersoraffordabilityconstraints.Furthermore,theconventionalapproach tobuildingenergyresearch,detailedon-siteauditsandphysicalsimulationmodels,isresource- intensive,makingitdifficulttoextendanalysistothefullnationalhousingstock,particularlyin 3 areaswithlimiteddatainfrastructurewheretheconsequencesofthermalinequalityareoftenmost severe. Recentadvancesinmachinelearning(ML),deeplearning(DL),andtherapidexpansionof urban-scaleobservationaldatapresentnewopportunitiestoaddressthesegaps.Agrowingbody ofworkdemonstratesthatimage-basedanddata-drivenapproachescanpredictdiversebuilding energy-relatedattributes(AmasyaliandEl-Gohary,2018).Mayeretal.(2023)combinestreetview imagery(SVI)andaerialviewimagery(AVI)topredictbuildingenergyefficiency.Streltsovetal. (2020)predictenergyconsumptionfromaerialimageryalone.Rosenfelderetal.(2021)similarly predictelectricityconsumptionusingSVIandAVI.Beyondindividualbuildingattributes,high- resolutionsmartmeterdatanowenablesthecharacterisationofdomesticelectricityuseatnational scale,revealinghowstructuralinefficiencyandsocio-economicdisadvantageareexpressedinthe temporalrhythmsofhouseholdenergyconsumption.Together,thesemethodologicaltoolsprovide ascalablepathwayforidentifyingthermallyvulnerablehousing,understandingthestructural driversofdoublejeopardy,andultimatelysupportingmoreequitabledecarbonisationstrategies. Followingthisbackground,thisresearchisorganisedaroundtheoverarchingquestionofhow doublethermaljeopardycanbeidentified,characterised,andunderstoodacrosstheUKhousing stockusingscalable,data-drivenmethods.Morespecifically,thisdissertationaddressesthefollowing researchquestions: •HowcanHard-to-Decarbonisebuildingsbeidentifiedatscaleusingmulti-modalurban dataanddeeplearning? •Whatisthespatialdistributionandmagnitudeofheatlossacrossthehousingstock,including buildingswithoutformalenergyperformancerecords? •Whatarethecharacteristicsandstructuraldeterminantsofindoorheatstressindomestic environmentsduringsummer? •Howareresidentialelectricityuserhythmsstructuredbybuildingefficiencyandsocio- economiccontext,andwhatdotheyrevealaboutthetemporaldimensionofdoublethermal jeopardy?

sections.heading: Background
sections.text: 16 2.2.2DomesticDeterminantsofThermalExperience Thermalcomfortinhomesisco-producedbyphysiological,behavioural,andbuilt-environment determinants(DeDearandBrager,2002;ISO,2025b).Physiologicalsensitivityvarieswithage, healthstatus,andhydration;olderadultsandindividualswithchronicillnessoftenexhibitreduced thermoregulatorycapacity.Behaviourally,comfortdependsonexpectations,habituation,andthe perceivedabilitytomodifytheenvironmentsuchasadjustingheating,openingwindows,orusing shadingdevices. Critically,buildingandsystemcharacteristicsstronglymediatetheseadaptiveopportunities. Insulationandairtightnessreducewinterheatlossbutmayalsolimitpassivecoolinginsummerif notpairedwitheffectiveventilationstrategies.Thermalmass,glazingarea,orientation,andshading influencesolargainsandpeaktemperatures,whileventilationstrategiesshapebothindoorair qualityandheatremovalcapacity.EmpiricalUKmonitoringstudiesdemonstratethatoverheating prevalenceisassociatedwithbuiltform,retrofitmeasures,orientation,andoccupantbehaviour, highlightingthecomplexinteractionbetweenfabricupgradesandsummertimerisk(Zahiriand Gupta,2023). Inpractice,domesticcomfortassessmentcombinesinstrumentedmonitoring,airtemperature, surfacetemperature,operativetemperature,humidity,airvelocity,andoftenCOasaproxyfor 2 ventilation,withsubjectiveoccupantfeedback(ISO,2025a).Operativetemperature,whichinte- gratesairandradianteffects,isfrequentlyusedinoverheatingevaluationagainstTM59thresholds (Cibse,2017).Theintegrationofobjectiveandsubjectivemeasuresunderscoresthatthermal comfortissimultaneouslyphysicalandexperiential. 2.2.3ColdHomes,Overheating,andtheUKPolicyContext IntheUK,thermalcomfortisembeddedwithinbroaderpolicyframeworksaddressingbothcold homesandoverheatingrisk.FuelpovertyisformallydefinedthroughtheLowIncomeLowEnergy Efficiency(LILEE)indicator,whichlinksenergyefficiencybandthresholdstoresidualhousehold incomeafterrequiredheatingexpenditure(DepartmentforEnergySecurityandNetZero,2025). 2.3“DoubleJeopardy”andThermalMinorities 17 Thisdefinitionexplicitlyrecognisesthatadequatewinterwarmthisconstrainedbybothbuilding performanceandaffordability. Simultaneously,nationalhousingstatisticsindicatethatoverheatingisagrowingconcern.The EnglishHousingSurveyreportsthat12%ofhouseholdsin2024experiencedatleastonepartof theirhomeas“uncomfortablyhot,”withhigherreportinginsomenewerdwellings(Ministry ofHousing,CommunitiesandLocalGovernment,2026).Thischallengestheassumptionthat improvedfabricefficiencyautomaticallyguaranteescomfortacrossseasons. PublichealthguidancefromtheUKHealthSecurityAgency(UKHSA)andtheNHSfurther emphasiseshousehold-levelcoolingbehaviours,shading,ventilationtiming,andfanuse,highlight- ingthatvulnerabilityduringheatwavesisunevenlydistributedacrosspopulationgroups(National HealthService,2022;UKHealthSecurityAgency,2025).TheWorldHealthOrganizationsimilarly framesbothlowandhighindoortemperaturesashealthrisks,situatingthermalcomfortwithina broaderhousing-and-healthagenda(Organization,2018). 2.3“DoubleJeopardy”andThermalMinorities 2.3.1DoubleJeopardyorigins DoublejeopardyisaconceptthatdatesbacktoRomanlawandwaslaterincorporatedintoEnglish commonlaw.IntheUnitedStates,thisprotectionisenshrinedintheFifthAmendmentofthe Constitution,whichstates: “...norshallanypersonbesubjectforthesameoffencetobetwiceputinjeopardyoflifeor limb”. Althoughdoublejeopardyisprimarilyalegaldoctrinedefinedthroughconstitutionaltextand judicialinterpretation,thetermhasalsobeenwidelyadoptedinsociologicalresearch.Forexample, Williamsetal.(2014)arguesthatwomenofcolourexperiencecompoundedformsofdiscrimination duetotheirsimultaneousmembershipinmultiplemarginalisedgroups.Thisintersectionofrace andgendercanaddtoormultiplydisadvantage,ratherthanoperatingasseparateandindependent formsofbias(Almquist,1975).

sections.text: 3.1Framinganunderstandingofdoublethermaljeopardy TheprecedingChapter2establishedthatUKhomesfaceastructurallyembedded,bidirectional thermalchallenge.Wintercoldexposureandsummerheatstressarenotindependenthazards buttwomanifestationsofasharedunderlyingcondition:ahousingstockthat,initscurrent form,inadequatelymediatesbetweenoccupantsandclimaticextremes.Theconceptof double isintroducedheretonamethiscompoundedcondition.Ahouseholdindouble thermaljeopardy thermaljeopardyisonewherethesamestructuralconstraintsthatdrivewinterheatloss,poor insulation,hard-to-retrofitbuildingfabric,restrictedadaptivecapacity,alsoheightensusceptibility toindooroverheatingduringsummer.Ratherthanfacingthermalriskinasingleseason,these householdsareexposedacrosstheyear,withvulnerabilitydeepenedbytheinteractionbetween buildingperformance,socio-economicdisadvantage,andanincreasinglyvolatileclimate. Thiscompoundinglogichasimportantimplicationsforhowtheproblemisunderstoodand studied.Muchoftheexistingliteraturetreatswintercoldandsummeroverheatingasseparate issues,eachwithitsownmeasures,policies,andresearchapproaches.AsreviewedinChapter2, thisseasonalsplithidesthehouseholdshithardest:thosewhosehomesaretoocoldinwinter anddangerouslyhotinsummer.Thisseparationalsocreatesgapsinresearchmethods.Energy efficiencyratingsmeasurehowmuchheatabuildingloses,butdonotcaptureoverheatingrisk; equally,overheatingassessmentsrarelyconsiderstructuralbarrierstoimprovementorfuelpoverty. Conceptualframework 38 Aframeworkthatcoversbothheatextremes,whileplacingthemwithinthecontextofsocialand spatialinequality,isthereforeneeded. Thepurposeofthischapteristwofold:toarticulatethestructurallogicthroughwhichdouble thermaljeopardyarises,andtoestablishtheanalyticalarchitecturethroughwhichitcanbeempir- icallyexaminedatscale.Theframeworkdoesnottreatthermalvulnerabilityasafixedattribute ofbuildingsorhouseholds,butasanemergentpropertyoftheirinteraction,shapedbybuilding fabric,urbanexposure,energybehaviour,andthesocio-economiccontextthatconditionsadaptive capacity.Inthissense,doublethermaljeopardyisnotmerelyatechnicalconditionbutaformof structuralinequality,reproducedthroughhousingsystemsthatremaindifficulttodecarbonise anddifficulttocool. Theanalyticalframeworkproceedsthroughfourinterrelatedmodules,aselaboratedinSec- tion3.3.ThefirstmoduleestablishesthestructuralfoundationbyidentifyingHard-to-Decarbonise (HtD)buildings,thosewhosephysicalandmorphologicalcharacteristicspresentintrinsicbarriers toretrofit.Thesecondmodulemapsthespatiallandscapeofheatloss,extendingefficiencyclas- sificationtotheportionofthebuildingstockwithoutformalenergyperformancerecords.The thirdmoduleexaminestheunderexploredsummerdimensionofdoublejeopardybycharacterising indoorheatstressconditionsduringheatwaveevents,situatingthisexposurewithinbuildingand socio-economicdeterminants.Thefourthmoduleconnectsboththermalextremestothetemporal structureofdomesticelectricityuse,revealinghowstructuralinefficiencyandsocio-economiccon- straintareexpressedthroughpatternsofenergyconsumptionthatpersistacrossseasons.Together, thesemodulesoperationaliseanunderstandingofdoublethermaljeopardyasaconditionthatisat oncephysical,social,andtemporal. 3.2Methods Tobetteraddressthemethodologicalgapincurrentstudiesandsynthesisetheanalyticalmethods proposedforthisresearch,wepresentsomemethodologicalconceptsandfoundationsofthis research,inordertobetterillustratethefeasibilityofthewholedissertation.Morespecifically,the concept,process,andapplicationofthetechniquesareexplainedtoprovideabasicunderstanding 3.2Methods 39 Figure3.1ModelarchitectureforagenericCNNmodel ofthemethods.Keytechniquesinvolvedinthisresearchincludeconvolutionalneuralnetworks andVisionTransformers,explainablemachinelearning,thermalcomfortmetrics,andtime-series analysis. 3.2.1VisionArtificialIntelligence LeCunetal.(1995)introducedtheConvolutionalNeuralNetwork(CNN),aclassofdeepfeed- forwardneuralnetworksthathasdemonstratedstrongcapabilityinextractingspatiallystructured features.CNNshavebecomefoundationalintaskssuchastimeseriesanalysis,imagerecognition, objectdetection,andimageclassification. TheoverallarchitectureofCNNsencompassesconvolutionallayers,poolinglayers,andfully connectedlayers,designedtomodelcomplexhigh-dimensionaldata.CNNstypicallyconsist ofmultiplehierarchicalstacksofconvolutionandpoolingoperations.Theconvolutionallayer establisheslocalconnectivitybyapplyinglearnablefiltersthatslideacrosstheinputdata.Through weightsharing,CNNsefficientlycapturelocalspatialpatternswhilemaintainingcomputational tractability.Eachconvolutionoperationproducesafeaturemap,followedbynonlinearactiva- tion,enablingthenetworktolearnincreasinglyabstractrepresentations.Poolinglayersreduce spatialresolutionandenhancetranslationinvariance,whilefullyconnectedlayersperformfinal classificationorregressiontasks. Conceptualframework 40 Inbuildingenergyresearch,CNNsareparticularlysuitedforextractingfaçade-levelcharac- teristicsfromstreet-viewimageryorremotesensingdata.Featuressuchaswindow-to-wallratio, shadingdevices,buildingheightproxies,materialtexture,andfaçadearticulationcanbelearned implicitlyfrompixel-levelpatterns.Inthepilotstudy,CNN-basedmodelsdemonstratedhigh predictiveaccuracyandcomputationalefficiencyinenergyratingclassificationtasks,validating theirabilitytocapturespatiallylocalmorphologicalcuesrelevanttothermalperformance. However,CNNsrelyprimarilyonlocalreceptivefields.Whiledeeperarchitecturesexpand effectivereceptiverange,long-rangedependenciesandglobalcontextualrelationshipsarenot explicitlymodelled.Inthecontextofbuildingenergyperformance,suchglobalrelationshipscan becritical.Thermalriskandenergyefficiencyareshapednotonlybylocalfaçadecomponentsbut alsobybroadercompositionalpatterns,volumetricconfiguration,urbancontext,andsocio-spatial relationships. Toaddresstheselimitations,thisresearchfurtherincorporatesTransformer-basedarchitectures, originallyintroducedinnaturallanguageprocessing(Vaswanietal.,2017)andsubsequentlyadapted forvisiontasksthroughVisionTransformers(ViT).UnlikeCNNs,Transformersrelyonaself- attentionmechanismthatallowseachelementoftheinputtoattendtoeveryotherelement.In visionapplications,imagesaredividedintopatches,whicharetreatedastokens.Self-attention computespairwiseinteractionsbetweenpatches,enablingthemodeltolearnglobaldependencies directly. Theattentionmechanismcanbeformulatedas: (cid:18)(cid:19) TQK AttentionSoftmax√ (Q,K,V)=V d k where,,andrepresentquery,key,andvaluematricesderivedfrominputembeddings, QKV andisthedimensionalityofthekeyvectors.Thisformulationallowsthemodeltodynamically d k weightrelationshipsacrosstheentireimage,capturingbothlocalandglobalstructures. Forbuildingenergyapplications,Transformer-basedmodelsofferseveraladvantages: •Abilitytomodelrelationshipsbetweendistantfaçadecompo- Globalspatialreasoning: nents(e.g.,roofconfigurationandground-floorglazing). 3.2Methods 41 •Improvedrepresentationofbuildingmassingandvolumetricpropor- Contextsensitivity: tions. •Naturalcompatibilitywithstructuredmetadata(e.g.,building Multimodalintegration: age,height,IndexofMultipleDeprivation(IMD)score)throughtoken-basedfusion. •Strongperformancewhentrainedonlarge-scaleurbandatasets. Scalability: Inthisdissertation,CNNsareemployedprimarilyinearly-stagefeatureextractionandpilot experimentsduetotheircomputationalefficiencyandrobustness.Transformer-basedarchitectures areintroducedinsubsequentstagestoenhanceglobalcontextualmodellingandenablemultimodal integrationbetweenfaçadeimageryandstructuredsocio-technicaldata.Thisprogressionreflects thebroadermethodologicalshiftincomputervisionfrompurelyconvolutionalrepresentations towardattention-basedglobalreasoning. ByintegratingbothCNNandTransformerparadigms,theframeworkleverageslocalmorpho- logicalfeatureextractionalongsideglobalrelationalmodelling.Thishybridapproachisparticularly suitedtobuildingenergyanalysis,wherethermalperformanceemergesfrominteractionsbetween fabric,form,andsocio-economiccontextratherthanisolatedfaçadeelements. 3.2.2ExplainableModellingFramework Toensureinterpretabilityacrossheterogeneousmodellingtasks,thisresearchadoptsamulti-level explainablemodellingframework.Ratherthantreatingpredictionasanendinitself,modelsare usedtoidentifystructuraldriversofthermalvulnerabilityandenergybehaviour.Threecomple- mentaryapproachesareemployed:regressionanalysis,explainableensemblelearning(Random ForestwithSHAP),andvisualexplanationofdeeplearningmodels(Grad-CAM). RegressionAnalysis:ParametricAttribution Regressionmodelsareusedtoquantifyassociationsbetweenindoorheatstressindicatorsand explanatoryvariablesincludingoutdoormeteorologicalconditions,buildingcharacteristics,and socio-economiccontext. Conceptualframework 42 Thisapproachprovidesdirectionalinterpretation,statisticalsignificancetestingandexplicit modellingoflageffectsandinteractionterms.Regressionanalysisservesasatheory-alignedbaseline model,enablingtransparentestimationofhowspecificfactors—suchasEPCrating,dwellingtype, tenure,ordeprivation—areassociatedwithindoorthermalconditions. RandomForestwithSHAP:NonlinearandInteractionEffects Tocapturenonlinearrelationshipsandhigher-orderinteractionsamongbuildingphysicalattributes andsocio-economicvariables,RandomForestmodelsareemployed.Unlikeparametricregression, RandomForestdoesnotimposefunctionalformassumptionsandcanflexiblymodelcomplex dependencies. Tomaintaininterpretability,SHapleyAdditiveexPlanations(SHAP)areappliedtoquantify featurecontributionstomodelpredictions.SHAPvaluesprovideagame-theoreticmeasureof marginalcontributionforeachvariable,allowingtheglobalimportancerankingoffeaturesand cluster-specificattributionanalysis. Grad-CAM:VisualExplanationofDeepLearning Forimage-baseddeeplearningmodelsusedinbuildingenergyandretrofitclassification,Gradient- weightedClassActivationMapping(Grad-CAM)isemployedtovisualisediscriminativeregions withininputimagery. Grad-CAMgeneratesspatialheatmapsbycomputinggradientsofthetargetclasswithrespectto convolutionalfeaturemaps,highlightingimageregionsthatmoststronglyinfluenceclassification decisions.Thisallowsexaminationofwhetherthemodelattendstoarchitecturallymeaningful elements,suchasroofstructures,chimneys,windowsandfaçadematerials.Bylinkingmodel attentiontointerpretablebuildingfeatures,Grad-CAMprovidesevidencethatpredictionsare groundedinphysicallyplausiblearchitecturalcharacteristicsratherthanspuriouscorrelations. Together,thesethreeapproachesestablishalayeredinterpretabilityframework: Theintegrationoftheabovemethodsensuresthateachmodellingstage—thermalstressdetec- tion,energyrhythmclustering,andbuildingefficiencyclassification—remainstransparentand theoreticallygrounded.Ratherthantreatingmachinelearningasablackbox,theframeworkpri- 3.2Methods 43 oritisesexplanatoryclarity,aligningpredictivemodellingwiththedissertation’sbroaderobjective ofrevealinghowstructuralbuildingconditionsandsocio-economicconstraintsshapeunequal thermaloutcomes. 3.2.3ThermalComfortMeasurement Tocharacteriseindoorthermalcomfortconditionsduringsummerheatevents,twocomplementary metricsareemployed:apsychrometricanalysisframeworkandtheHeatIndex(HI).Together,these toolsenableanintegratedassessmentofthecombinedeffectsofairtemperatureandhumidityon humanthermalperception,whileremaininginterpretableandsuitableforsensor-basedmonitoring andscalableanalysis. Thepsychrometricchartisusedtosituatemeasuredindoorenvironmentalconditionsrelative toestablishedthermalcomfortzones.Apsychrometricchartrepresentsthethermodynamic propertiesofmoistair,enablingsimultaneousexaminationofdry-bulbtemperature,relative humidity,humidityratio,andenthalpy. Comfortzonesdefinedbyrecognisedstandards,suchasASHRAEStandard55,areoverlaid toassesswhetherrecordedindoorstatesfallwithinoroutsideacceptablecomfortranges.This approachprovidesaphysicallygroundedrepresentationofhowtemperatureandmoisturejointly influenceperceivedcomfort,movingbeyondsingle-variablethresholds.Itisparticularlysuitable forindoorenvironments,wherehumiditysignificantlymodulatesevaporativeheatdissipationand thereforethermalstress. Environmentalparametersusedintheanalysisincludedry-bulbtemperature,relativehumidity andairvelocity.Dry-bulbtemperatureandrelativehumiditywereobtaineddirectlyfromindoor sensormeasurements.Airvelocityisassumedtobe0.1m/s,consistentwithlowair-movement conditionstypicalofresidentialindoorenvironments,followingASHRAEguidance(ASHRAE, 2020). Measureddatapointsareprojectedontothepsychrometricchart,withdry-bulbtemperature representedalongthex-axisandhumidityratioalongthey-axis.Linesofconstantrelativehumidity andenthalpyareusedtoaidinterpretationofthermalstates.Allvisualisationsaregeneratedusing thePythonlibrary,ensuringreproducibilityandconsistencyacrossdwellings. psychrochart Conceptualframework 44 Whilethepsychrometricchartprovidesanalyticaldepth,theHeatIndex(HI)offersasimpli- fiedandcommunicableindicatorofperceivedthermaldiscomfort.TheHeatIndexcombines airtemperatureandrelativehumiditytoapproximatethermalstressexperiencedbyoccupants, particularlyunderhotandhumidconditionswhereevaporativecoolingbecomesconstrained. Althoughoriginallydevelopedforoutdoormeteorologicalapplications,theHeatIndexhasbeen widelyadoptedasaproxyforindoorheatdiscomfortduringextremeheatevents.Itscomputa- tionalsimplicitymakesitwellsuitedforhigh-temporal-resolutionsensordataandeventdetection algorithms. TheHeatIndexwascomputedusingtheformulationproposedbyRothfuszandHeadquarters (1990).TemperaturerecordedindegreesCelsiuswasconvertedtodegreesFahrenheitpriorto calculation: HI =−42.379+2.04901523T+10.14333127RH−0.22475541TRH FF −32−22−6.83783×10T−5.481717×10RH F −32−42+1.22874×10TRH+8.5282×10TRH FF −622−1.99×10TRH F wheredenotesairtemperatureindegreesFahrenheitandrepresentsrelativehumidity TRH F expressedasapercentage. ThecombineduseofthepsychrometricchartandtheHeatIndexallowsbothphysicallyrigorous evaluationandaccessibleinterpretation.Thepsychrometricframeworkcapturesthethermody- namicstateofindoorairandsituatesitrelativetoestablishedcomfortstandards,whiletheHeat Indextranslatestemperature–humidityinteractionsintoaperceptuallymeaningfulstressindicator. Thisdual-metricstrategyisparticularlyimportantintheUKcontext,whereoverheatingassess- mentremainslessinstitutionalisedthanwinterheat-lossevaluation.Byintegratingthermodynamic representationwithaperceptualstressproxy,theframeworksupportstransparentandscalable assessmentofindoorheatstressacrossdiversehousingconditions. 3.2Methods 45 3.2.4Time-SeriesAnalysisofThermalandEnergyDynamics Acoremethodologicalpillarofthisresearchistheanalysisofhigh-resolutiontime-seriesdatato characterisedomesticthermalconditionsandelectricityuseasdynamicprocessesratherthanstatic quantities.Twocomplementaryapproachesaredeveloped:(1)anindoormini-heatwavedetection algorithmbasedonthermalsensordata,and(2)clusteringofelectricityloadprofilestoidentify recurringenergy-userhythms.Together,thesemethodsestablishaunifiedtemporalanalytical frameworkforunderstandinghowhouseholdsexperienceandrespondtothermalstress. IndoorMini-HeatwaveDetection Indooroverheatingisconceptualisedasatemporaleventratherthanasingletemperatureex- ceedance.Tooperationalisethis,anindoor“mini-heatwave”isdefinedasasustainedperiodduring whichindoortemperatureexceedsaspecifiedcomfortthresholdforaminimumduration. Usingminute-levelindoorsensordata,thealgorithmproceedsasfollows: 1.Atemperaturethreshold(e.g.,26C)isdefinedbasedonrecognised◦ ThresholdDefinition: comfortstandards. 2.Consecutivetimestepsexceedingthethresholdmustpersistfora DurationConstraint: predefinedminimumperiodtoqualifyasanevent. 3.Rapidtemperatureincreasesareidentifiedtodistinguishsustained Slope-BasedFiltering: overheatingfromshort-termfluctuations. 4.Shortinterruptionsbelowthethresholdaremergedwhereappropriateto GapMerging: capturecontinuousheataccumulation. Thisevent-basedapproachenablescomparisonbetweenindooroverheatingepisodesandofficial outdoorheatwavedeclarations.Byfocusingonduration,persistence,andthermalaccumulation, thealgorithmcapturesthelivedtemporalexperienceofindoorheatstress,whichmaynotalign withmeteorologicaldefinitions. Conceptualframework 46 ElectricityRhythmRepresentationandClustering Inparallel,householdelectricitydemandisanalysedasarecurringtemporalrhythmderivedfrom half-hourlysmartmeterdata.Ratherthanrelyingonannualormonthlytotals,electricitycon- sumptionistransformedintoarepresentativeaverageweeklyprofile(487resolution),preserving × systematicintra-dayandweekday–weekendpatternswhilesmoothingirregularfluctuations. Tocaptureheterogeneityinenergypractices,atwo-stepclusteringframeworkisemployed.In thefirststep,householdsaregroupedbasedontotalelectricityconsumption VolumeClustering: magnitude;inthesecondstep,normalisedweeklyloadprofilesareclustered PatternClustering: toidentifydistincttemporalusagestyles Byseparatingmagnitudeandtemporalstructure,thisframeworkdistinguisheshouseholdsthat consumesimilaramountsofelectricitybutexhibitfundamentallydifferentdailyrhythms. Althoughappliedtodifferentdatasets,bothmethodsshareacommonanalyticallogic:theytreat thermalcomfortandelectricitydemandastemporallystructuredphenomena.Themini-heatwave algorithmidentifiesdiscreteoverheatingeventscharacterisedbypersistenceandaccumulationover time.Therhythmclusteringframeworkidentifiesstable,recurringenergy-usepatternsthatreflect dailyroutinesandstructuralconstraints.Byintegratingeventdetectionandrhythmclassification withinasharedtime-seriesframework,thisresearchmovesbeyondstaticindicatorsofenergy inefficiencyortemperatureexceedance.Instead,itconceptualisesdomesticthermalvulnerability asemergingfromdynamicinteractionsbetweenclimaticexposure,buildingperformance,and temporallystructuredenergypractices. 3.3Analyticalframework Inthelastsection,wepresentedthekeytechniquesusedintheframework;inthissection,we illustratethedetailsoftheanalyticalframeworkofourresearch.Wedesignananalyticalframework toidentify,understand,andprofilethedoublyjeopardisedsituationoftheUKdomesticbuildings asshowninFigure3.2. 3.3Analyticalframework 47 Figure3.2Analyticalframework Conceptualframework 48 3.3.1Multi-SourceUrbanDatasetandDataIntegrationFramework TheUnitedKingdomprovidesoneofthemostcomprehensivedigitalinfrastructuresforbuild- ingenergyandthermalcomfortresearch.Overhalfofthedomesticbuildingstockpossessesan EnergyPerformanceCertificate(EPC),whichdocumentsbuildingmorphology,fabriccharacteris- tics,heatingsystems,lightingefficiency,insulationquality,andothertechnicalattributes.Each recordincludesacalculatedenergyefficiencyscoreandrating(A–G),derivedfromtheStandard AssessmentProcedure(SAP). WhileEPCdataarenotwithoutlimitations,includingtemporallag,assessorvariability,and model-basedestimationbias,theyremainoneofthemostextensivenational-scalebuildingen- ergydatasetsavailableinternationally.Theircoverageenableslarge-scalestatisticalmodellingand machinelearningapplicationsacrossheterogeneoushousingtypologies. However,datarichnessintroducesanalyticalcomplexity.Informationrelevanttobuilding thermalperformanceisdistributedacrossmultiplesources,spatialscales,andformats.Asillustrated inFigure3.3,thisresearchintegratessevenprimarydatalayers,spanningremotesensing,street-level imagery,buildinggeometry,andenergyconsumptionrecords. Thermalsatelliteimagerycaptureslandsurfacetemperaturepatterns, RemoteSensingLayers. providingspatialproxiesforurbanheatexposureandsurfacethermalbehaviour.Standardopti- calsatelliteimagerysuppliescontextualinformationonroofmaterial,vegetationcoverage,and surroundingurbanfabric. Streetviewfaçadeimagesprovidefine-grainedvisualinformation Street-LevelObservations. onbuildingenvelopecharacteristics,includingwindowconfiguration,materiality,shadingdevices, façadearticulation,andvisibleretrofitinterventions.Inselectedcasestudies,thermalstreet-level imagerysupplementsthisdataset,enablingsurfaceheat-lossdetectionandenvelopeperformance analysis. TheUKBuildingsdatasetprovidesdetailedbuilding BuildingMorphologyandFootprints. footprintsandgeometricrepresentations.Theseserveasthespatialbackboneoftheintegration framework,enablinglinkagebetweengeospatiallayersandproperty-levelrecords.Morphological 3.3Analyticalframework 49 Figure3.3Multi-layerdataarchitectureintegratingremotesensing,façadeimagery,morphology, andenergyconsumptiondata. Conceptualframework 50 attributessuchasheightproxies,volumeestimations,andadjacencyrelationshipsarederivedfrom thislayer. EPCrecordsprovidemodelledenergyeffi- EnergyPerformanceandConsumptionData. ciencyindicatorsandfabric-leveldescriptors.Smartmeterdatasupplyhigh-resolutiontemporal electricityconsumptionprofiles,capturingbehaviouralandoperationaldimensionsofenergyuse. Together,theselayersbridgepredictedandobservedperformance. Dataintegrationisachievedthroughspatialalignmentand SpatialIntegrationviaUPRN. property-levelidentifiers.TheUniquePropertyReferenceNumber(UPRN)providesapersistent identifierlinkingaddress-levelrecordsacrossdatasets.EPCandbuildingfootprintdataarejoined viaUPRN,ensuringproperty-levelconsistency.Satelliteandstreet-viewimageryareretrieved basedongeoreferencedbuildingfootprints.SmartmeterdataareassociatedwithselectedUPRNs, enablingdirectlinkagebetweenconsumptionpatternsandphysicalbuildingattributes. Thislocation-basedjoiningstrategyestablishesaunifiedanalyticalframeworkinwhicheach propertyisrepresentedasaverticallyintegrateddatastack,fromsubsurfacestreetnetworksto buildinggeometry,façadeappearance,andatmosphericexposure.Bystructuringthedatasetin thislayeredmanner,theframeworksupportsmulti-scalemodellingofthermalrisk,enablingthe analysisofhowbuildingfabric,morphology,behaviouralenergyuse,andenvironmentalexposure jointlyshapeunequalthermaloutcomes. Ratherthantreatingeachdatasetinisolation,thisstudyconceptualisestheurbanbuildingasa multi-dimensionaldataobjectembeddedwithinabroadersocio-technicalandclimaticsystem. Theintegrationoftheselayersprovidestheempiricalfoundationforsubsequentcomputervision, machinelearning,andspatialmodellingcomponentsofthedissertation. 3.3.2Identification:Multi-ModalLearningfromBuildings ThefirstanalyticalmoduleestablishesascalableframeworkforidentifyingHard-to-Decarbonise (HtD)buildingsusingmulti-sourceurbandata(Figure3.4).Ratherthanrelyingondetailed buildingauditsorsimulation-basedmodelling,thismoduletreatsHtDidentificationasapattern- recognitionproblemgroundedinobservablephysicalandenvironmentalcharacteristics. 3.3Analyticalframework 51 Inthefirstcomponent,wepreparethemulti-modalrepresentationfromthedatasetmentioned inSection3.3.1andtrainthemodelbasedonthedatarepresentation.WefirstdefinetheHtD statusofthebuilding.HtDstatusisoperationalisedusingrule-basedcriteriaderivedfromEnergy PerformanceCertificate(EPC)records.BuildingsarelabelledasHtDiftheyexhibitstructural characteristicsassociatedwithretrofitdifficulty,suchassolidwallconstruction,flatroofs,heritage typologies,orhard-to-fillcavitywalls.Thistransformationofaudit-leveldescriptorsintoabinary labelenablesasupervisedlearningsetup.Eachbuildingisthenrepresentedasaverticallyintegrated dataobjectcomposedofStreetViewImagery(façade-levelenvelopecharacteristics),AerialIm- agery(roofformandcontextualmorphology),LandSurfaceTemperature(thermalproxy)and Morphologicaldescriptorsderivedfrombuildingfootprints.Thisrepresentationallowsretrofitdif- ficultytobeinferredfrombothvisualsignaturesandspatialattributes,withoutrequiringintrusive inspectiondata. ThesecondcomponentisLarge-ScaleInferenceforBuildingsWithoutEPC.IntheUKcontext, asubstantialshareofthebuildingstocklacksvalidEPCrecords.Toaddressthiscoveragegap, thetrainedmodelisappliedtobuildingswithoutEPClabels,whichaccountforapproximately 40%ofthestockinthestudyareas.Throughthisinferencestep,energyefficiencyisestimatedfor thepreviouslyunobservedportionoftheurbanfabric.Thisexpandstheanalyticalscopefrom alabelledsubsettoanear-completebuildinginventory,enablingcity-scalemappingofenergy performance.Predictionsaresubsequentlyaggregatedfromindividualbuildingstoadministrative units(datazones)tosupportspatialanalysisandpolicyinterpretation. ThethirdcomponentisCharacterisingBuildingsandNeighbourhoods.Thefinalstepinvesti- gatesthestructuralandsocio-spatialcharacteristicsassociatedwithpredictedenergyperformance. Atthebuildinglevel,relationshipsareexaminedbetweenefficiencyclassificationandmorphological attributes(e.g.,form,rooftype,density),thermalsignatures,andexternalenvelopecharacteristics. Attheneighbourhoodlevel,predictedenergyperformanceisspatiallyjoinedwithdeprivation indicators(e.g.,SIMD)toexploreco-occurrencepatternsbetweenenergyinefficiencyandsocio- Conceptualframework 54 economicvulnerability.Thisstepmovestheanalysisfrompredictiontointerpretation,examining howheatlosspatternsintersectwithurbanmorphologyandsocialstructure.Ratherthantreating energyinefficiencyasapurelytechnicalcondition,itsituatesthermalperformancewithinthe broadersocio-spatialfabricofthecity. Thismoduleextendstheanalysisfromstructuralretrofitdifficulty(Module1)toactualenergy performanceandheat-losspatterns.Itmapswhereinefficiencyisconcentratedandexamineshow itrelatestourbanformandneighbourhoodcontext.Together,thesestepslaythegroundwork forsubsequentinvestigationintohowstructuralconstraints,thermalperformance,andsocial vulnerabilityconvergetoproduceunequalthermaloutcomes. 3.3.4UnderstandingHeatStressinDomesticEnvironments WhilemuchoftheUK’sbuildingenergydiscoursehashistoricallycentredonwinterheatloss, risingsummertemperaturesincreasinglyexposedomesticenvironmentstooverheatingrisks.In contrasttotherelativelymaturedatainfrastructureforheatingperformance(e.g.,EPC),systematic monitoringofindoorheatstressremainslimited. Thismoduleaddressesthisgapbycombiningself-collectedhigh-resolutionindoorsensordata withmeteorologicalandsocio-technicaldatasetstoexamineheatstresswithindomesticenviron- mentsduringsummerheatwaveconditions(Figure3.6).Theobjectiveisnotonlytomeasure overheatingbuttoconceptualiseindoorheatstressasasocio-technicalphenomenonshapedby buildingcharacteristics,environmentalexposure,andadaptivecapacity. Theanalyticalframeworkconsistsofthreecomponents.Thefirstcomponentistoevaluatethe indoorthermalcomfort.Toevaluateindoorheatexposure,weemploytwocomplementarythermal comfortmetrics:thepsychrometricchartandtheHeatIndex.Minute-levelindoortemperature andrelativehumiditydata,collectedfromsensor-enhancedhousingsurveys,areprojectedontoa psychrometriccharttovisualisetherelationshipbetweenthermalstateandestablishedcomfort zones(e.g.,ASHRAEStandard55).Thisrepresentationallowsforanassessmentofhowfrequently andtowhatextentindoorconditionsdeviatefromacceptablecomfortranges.Inparallel,theHeat Indexiscalculatedtoestimateperceivedthermalstressbyintegratingtemperatureandhumidity. 3.3Analyticalframework 55 Figure3.6UnderstandingHeatStressinDomesticEnvironments Whileoriginallydevelopedforoutdoorconditions,itprovidesanintuitiveandcommunicable metricforindoorheatdiscomfortduringextremeweatherevents. Thesecondcomponentinvolvesindoormini-heatwavedefinitionanddetection.Existingheat- waveclassificationsintheUKaredefinedexclusivelyusingoutdoortemperaturethresholds.How- ever,outdoordefinitionsdonotnecessarilyreflectindoorthermalexperiences.Tobridgethis gap,thisstudyintroducestheconceptofanindoor“mini-heatwave”—ashortbutcontinuous periodduringwhichindoortemperaturesexceedadefinedcomfortthreshold.Anevent-detection algorithmisdevelopedtoidentifythesemini-heatwavesfromhigh-temporal-resolutionindoor sensordata.Thealgorithmincorporates: •Atemperaturethreshold(e.g.,26°C), •Aminimumdurationrequirement, •Slope-basedlogictocapturerapidtemperatureincreases, •Gap-mergingprocedurestoensurerobustness. Conceptualframework 56 Thisapproachallowsindooroverheatingeventstobedetectedindependentlyfromofficial outdoorheatwavedeclarations,revealingpotentiallageffectsandpersistentheataccumulation withinbuildings. Thethirdcomponentistoexplorethedeterminantsofindoorheatstress.Toexaminethedrivers ofindoorheatstress,aregressionframeworkisimplementedlinkingindoortemperaturetooutdoor meteorologicalconditions,buildingcharacteristicsandsocioeconomicstatus.Thismodellingstep movesbeyonddetectiontowardexplanation.Itevaluateshowstructuralattributes,environmental exposure,andsocio-economicfactorsjointlyshapeindoorthermalconditions.Importantly,this analysisrevealsthetensionbetweenwinter-orientedenergyefficiencyimprovementsandsummer overheatingrisks.Buildingsdesignedtominimiseheatlossmayalsoreducenaturalventilationand heatdissipationcapacity,increasingsusceptibilitytoindoorheatstressduringextremesummer conditions. Thismodulecomplementstheheat-lossanalysisbyaddressingtheoppositeseasonalextreme.If Module2mapsthelandscapeofwinterinefficiency,thismoduleinvestigatessummeroverheat- ingandadaptivevulnerability.Together,theyestablishabidirectionalunderstandingofthermal risk—capturingbothinsufficientheatinginwinterandexcessiveheataccumulationinsummer. ThisdualperspectiveestablishesthatdomesticthermalvulnerabilityintheUKcannotbeunder- stoodthroughasingleseasonallens.Instead,itemergesfromtheinteractionbetweenbuilding fabric,climateexposure,andunequaladaptivecapacityacrossbothcoldandheatextremes. 3.3.5TemporalManifestationofDoubleThermalRisk Thefinalanalyticalmoduleexamineshowstructuralthermalvulnerabilityisexpressedthrough thetemporalstructureofdomesticelectricityuse(Figure3.7).Whileearliermodulesidentify buildinginefficiency(heatloss)andindooroverheating(heatstress),thismoduleinvestigateshow householdscopewiththeseconditionsthrougheverydayenergypractices. Usinghigh-resolutionhalf-hourlysmartmeterdataforapproximately20,000dwellings,electric- ityconsumptionisrepresentednotasaggregateannualdemandbutasaweeklytemporalrhythm. Eachhouseholdischaracterisedbybothconsumptionmagnitudeandload-shapestructure,en- 3.3Analyticalframework 57 Figure3.7TemporalManifestationofDoubleThermalRisk ablingtheidentificationofActivity-BasedHouseholdTypes(AHTs)thatdifferentiatebetween volumeintensityanddailytimingpatterns. Thismoduleproceedsinthreeanalyticalstages.Thefirstcomponentisrhythmrepresentation andclassification.Electricitydemandistransformedintorepresentative48×7weeklyprofiles, capturingpersistentbehaviouralpatternswhilesmoothingshort-termfluctuations.Atwo-step clusteringframeworkdistinguisheshouseholdsbytotalelectricityvolume(low,medium,high), andtemporalusagepatterns(Evening-Peaked,Midday-Active,Always-On).Thisseparationof magnitudeandrhythmallowstheidentificationofhouseholdsthatconsumesimilarquantitiesof electricitybutexhibitfundamentallydifferenttemporalstructures. ThesecondcomponentexploresStructuralandSocio-economicAttribution.RandomFor- estmodellingwithSHAP-basedexplainabilitylinkselectricityusestylestobuildingphysical attributesandsocio-economiccontext.Buildingefficiency(EPC),dwellingscale,anddeprivation gradientsareexaminedasstructuraldriversofbothconsumptionmagnitudeandtemporalper- sistence.Ratherthanattributingelectricitydemandsolelytobehaviouralchoice,thisframework situatestemporalenergypatternswithintheinteractionofbuildingfabric,spatialcapacity,and neighbourhood-leveldisadvantage. Thismoduledemonstratesthatinefficientandsocio-economicallydisadvantagedhouseholds aredisproportionatelyassociatedwithbothhigh-volumeandtemporallysustained(“Always-On”) electricitypatterns.Thissuggeststhatdoublethermalriskisnotonlyaconditionofpoorbuilding fabricandclimaticexposure,butalsooneoftemporalrigidity.Householdsexperiencingstructural inefficiencyandsocio-economicconstraintmayrelyoncontinuousenergyconsumptiontomain- tainbasicthermalcomfort,reducingtheirflexibilityunderelectrificationandpeak-pricingregimes. Byrevealinghowstructuralinequalityisembeddedinthetimingandpersistenceofelectricityuse, thismodulecompletestheanalyticalarcofthedissertation.Thermalvulnerabilityemergesnotonly frombuildingsandclimate,butfromtheinteractionbetweenphysicalinefficiency,socio-economic constraint,andtemporallystructuredenergypractices.

Currentresearchmainlyfocusesonproposingtechniquesfordecarbonisingthegeneralresidential (Baldwinetal.,2018;Xinetal.,2021)andcommercialbuildingsectors(Zhangetal.,2022b).This studydemonstratesthepredictivepowerofdeeplearningandopen-sourcedata.Inthisresearch,a methodologyforidentifyingHtDbuildingswithpubliclyavailabledataisproposedandtestedin Cambridge,UK.AdatasetofHtDhousesinCambridgeisorganized,withcriteriaderivedfromthe EPC,whichresultfromdetailedinspectionsofhouses.SVI,AVI,LST,andbuildingstockdataare usedtogetherforthepredictionwithdeeplearning.TheclassificationaccuracyforHtDbuildings achieves82%.Thisstudyalsoexplorestheminimaldataneededforthehigh-accuracypredictionof HtDhouses.ResultsshowthatSVIcontributesthemosttotheprediction. Therestofthischapterisorganisedasfollows:Section4.2reviewsrelatedworksinthesenseof conceptandcurrentresearchofHtDbuildingsandmethodsforpredictingbuilding-levelenergy attributes.Section4.3elaboratesonthedatausedandpre-processingofthedataprocedurein thisstudy.Section4.4entailstheprocessofthedata,modeltraining,andinterpretationand explorationoftheresults.Sectionpresentstheresultofourexperiment.Sectionpresentsthe ???? significance,possibleimplications,andlimitationsofthisstudy.Sectiondrawstheconclusions ?? ofthischapter. 4.2Background 4.2.1HtDhousingtowardssustainablecities TheconceptofHard-to-Decarbonize,traditionallylinkedtoheavyindustryactivities,hasbeen recentlyusedtodescribehouses.HtDhomesareestimatedtoberesponsibleforover25%ofall directresidentialsectoremissions(Fosteretal.,2019;RaslanandAmbrose,2022).Withtheurgency ofmakingthebuiltenvironmentsandcitiesmoresustainable,HtDbuildingalsodrawsattention fromgovernmentandpolicy-makers.In2022,theNationalHousingFederationandtheLocal GovernmentAssociationoftheUKpublishedareportnamed“HardtoDecarboniseSocialHomes” (Association,2022)statingtheemergencyandpotentialsolutionsforHtDsocialhomes.While thereportadmitsthatHtDhousesmaybedifferenttoeachother,thepotentialHtDbuildings couldstillbeselectedandidentifiedforbetterefficiency. DespitetheimportanceofsolvingHtDbuildingsproblems,thetopicisstilloflessinterestto researchers.Withahugeliteraturecoveringawiderangeofaspectsregardingthedecarbonisation 4.2Background 65 ofthebuildingstock,HtDbuildingshavenotreceivedenoughattention.Thecurrentstudyon HtDmainlyfocusesonproposingtechniquesfordecarbonisingthegeneralresidential(Baldwin etal.,2018;Xinetal.,2021)andcommercialbuildingsectors(Zhangetal.,2022b). 4.2.2Building-levelenergyperformanceprediction ThetaskofclassifyingHtDhousesshareslotsofsimilaritieswithotherbuildingenergy-related predictiontasks,suchasbuildingenergyconsumption,efficiency,andratings.Inthissubsection, wepresentreviewsofmethodsforbuilding-levelenergypredictions. Existingresearchforbuilding-levelenergy-relatedpredictionsmainlyappliesmethodsfrom engineeringcalculationandsimulation(Al-Homoud,2001;YaoandSteemers,2005),statistical modelling(BauerandScartezzini,1998),andmachinelearning(deRubeisetal.,2021;Seyedzadeh etal.,2018;Shaoetal.,2020;Zhaoetal.,2023).Engineeringtechniquesapplyfundamentalphysical principlestodeterminethermaldynamicsandenergycharacteristics.Thismethodisaccurateand effectivewhilerequiringdetailedinformationaboutthebuildingmodel,whichisinmanycases unavailable.Withtheincreasingavailabilityofvariousdatasources,data-drivenmethodssuchas statisticalmodellinganddeeplearninghavebeenappliedmoreandmorewidely.Thestatistical regressionmodelsestablishastraightforwardrelationshipbetweentheinfluencingvariablesand buildings’energy-relatedindex.Thestatisticalmodelsaremoreintuitiveandexplainable,while constrainedbytheabilitytosolvenon-linearrelationshipsbetweenvariablesandthetargetindex. Researchershavepreferredartificialneuralnetworksfortheirdependablepredictionsandability tosurmountthenonlinearitythatexistsbetweenenergy-relateddata’sinputandoutput(Kumar etal.,2013;Mohandesetal.,2019;Yuetal.,2016).Paudeletal.(2014)proposedabuildingheating demandpredictionmodelusingartificialneuralnetworksthatincorporatesoccupancyprofiles andoperationalheatingpowerlevelsinashort-termhorizon.Jetchevaetal.(2014)describeanew building-levelneuralnetwork-basedensemblemodelforday-aheadelectricityloadforecastingthat outperformsthecurrentbest-performingmodel(SARIMA)byupto50%incommercialand industrialsites. Despitethehighaccuracyachievedbypreviousstudiesinpredictingenergyusage,thesestudies requiregatheringsite-specificdata,suchashumanbehaviourfactors(e.g.,numberofoccupants Identification:Multi-ModalLearningfromBuildings 66 andheatingsetpointtemperature)andenergyconsumption-relatedindicators(e.g.,CO2emissions andsolarabsorptanceofwalls).Asthesesite-specificfactorsarebeyondcontrolinpractice,their applicabilityinretrofittingforgreenenergybuildingsislimitedduetotheirunavailability.In ordertoexpandourresearchtoawiderstudyareaandgainacomprehensiveunderstandingof buildingenergyefficiencyatthecitylevel,wewillexcludethosefactorsandinsteadfocusoneasily obtainableandwidelyavailablecharacteristics. 4.2.3Usingimagerydataforbuildingstockprediction Recentyearshavewitnessedariseinpredictingbuildingstockattributesfromimagedata.Among alltheimagedatasources,streetviewimageshavefoundextensiveapplicationinconjunction withdeeplearningtocomprehendconstructedsurroundings.Byemployingthesemethodologies, researchersexploreobjectswithlocationalsignificance(Zhangetal.,2019;Zhouetal.,2014), reconstructbuilding3Dmodels(PangandBiljecki,2022)andpredictbuildingage(Sunetal., 2022b). BesidespurelyrelyingonSVI,Mayeretal.(2023)useacombinationofSVI,AVIandinfrared LSTdatatopredictbuildingenergyefficiencyintheUK.Theend-to-enddeeplearningmethod achievesaprecisionof66%.Streltsovetal.(2020)predicthouseholdenergyconsumptionpurely withAVI.Buildingattributesareextractedfromsatelliteimagesandthenfedintodifferentmodels tomakethefinalprediction.Differentspatialaggregationlevelsarealsotestedtobalancethe granularityandpredictionperformance.Sunetal.(2022a)proposeafeature-fusionnetworkfor buildingenergyefficiencypredictionwithSVIandEPCdataandachievinganoverallprecisionof 86%. 4.3Data 4.3.1Studyarea ThisworktakesCambridge,UKasthestudysite.Asacitywithalonghistoryandalargenumber ofoldbuildings,itischallengingbutalsomeaningfultoidentifyandstudyHtDhouses.Besides 4.3Data 67 Table4.2DeterminationofHtD BuildingFeaturesDescription Hardtotreatwalltypesdonothavelow-costcavitywallinsulationoptionsavailableand/orpose Solidwalls technicaldifficultiesorrisksofpoorperformance. GSVimagescanbeaccessedthroughitsAPI,butthedefault SVIdownloadandpre-process viewisorientedtowardthestreet.Inordertoperformbuilding-specifictasks,animageofthe building’sfaçadeisnecessary.TheGSVAPIenablesthecustomisationofparameterssuchas heading,thefieldofview,andpitchtoadjustthecamera’sdirection,zoomlevel,andverticalangle. Here,wefollowthealgorithmproposedbySunetal.(2022b)forcalculatingcustomisedparameters forGSVAPI. WhileGSVimagesdownloadedareorientedtowardbuildingfacades,therearestillcertain samplesthatneedtobeexcluded.Theseincludeimageswherethebuildingappearstoosmalldue totheshootingpointbeingtoofaraway,aswellasimageswherethebuildingisobstructed.To addressthis,wesuggestanevaluationmethodthatutilizesaquantitativerepresentationofimage content.Theproposedmethodconsistsofthreesteps.Firstly,acandidateimageisinputtedintoa scene-parsingDCNNmodelthatassignssemanticcategorylabels(e.g.sky,building,tree)toevery pixelintheimage.WeuseasceneparsingmodeltrainedontheADE20Kdataset,whichincludes imageslabelledby150categories.Secondly,wecalculatetheratiosofeachvisualobjectintheimage. Lastly,weretainonlyimagesthatmeettwocriteria:thepercentageofbuildingcategoriesisthe highestamongallcategoriesandbuildingsoccupymorethan40%oftheimage. TheLSTdatadownloadingalgorithmdevelopedby(Ermidaetal.,2020)automatically LSTdata removedrastercellsthatarecoveredbycloudstoimprovethedataquality.Italsobringsupthe 4.4Method 71 issuethatforsomebuildings,LSTdataismissingforcertaincapturedates.Toavoidthis,monthly LSTdataisobtainedforJanuarytoDecember2022.TolinktheLSTdatawitheachindividual building,wecalculatezonalstatisticsforeachbuildingandLSTimage.Thecount,mean,variance andstandarddeviationarecomputed.Thesestatisticsarethenaveragedforeachbuilding,asMayer etal.(2023)indicatedthatitachievesbetterperformanceforprediction.Astheresult,LSTdata foreachbuildingisafour-dimensionalvectorcontainingthezonalstatisticsstatedabove. 4.4Method 4.4.1Modeldesign Withimagedata(SVIandAVI)andtabulardata(LSTandbuildingmorphologydescription),we designadeepconvolutionalneuralnetworkforclassifyingtheHtDhouses.Thismodelaimsat learningHtDhousinginformationsimultaneouslyfromdifferentattributes.AsshowninFigure 4.4,thenetworkmainlyconsistsoffourstages:input,featureextraction,featurefusion,and output.Intheinputandfeatureextractionstages,thenetworkrunsinthreeparallelbranches:two imagebranchesandatabularfeaturebranch.Theimagebranchestakebuildingfaçadephotos andaerialimagesasinput,withDenseConvolutionalNetwork(DenseNet)asthebackboneand outputtwo1024-dimensionaldeepfeaturesforfeaturefusion.Fourdenseblocksareusedinboth imagebranches.Forthetabularfeaturebranch,webuildasimplefully-connectedneuralnetwork withfourhiddenlayerswhichtakesfour-dimensiondata(LSTandbuildingmorphologicaldata) andyieldsa256-dimensionaldeepfeature.Inthefeaturefusionstage,thetwo1024-dimensional featuresfromtheimagebranchesandthe256-dimensionalfeaturefromthetabularfeaturebranch areconcatenatedasa2304-dimensionaldeepfeature.Thisconcatenateddeepfeatureisthenpassed intoafully-connectedneuralnetworkwithtwohiddenlayersandmakesthefinalclassificationof theHtDcategories. Formodelinitialisation,weapplydifferentstrategiesfortheimageandtabularfeaturebranches respectively.Theimagebranchesareinitializedwithweightspre-trainedontheImageNetdataset (Dengetal.,2009).ImageNetisawidelyuseddatasetfordetectingcommonobjectssuchas vehicles,buildings,andstreetsigns.Thepre-trainedweightsusedareabletounderstandandextract Identification:Multi-ModalLearningfromBuildings 72 Figure4.4Modelarchitecture informationaboutobjectsandsceneswithintheimages.Thisinitializationstrategyhelpsthefaster convergenceofthenetworkandrequireslesstrainingtime.Forthetabularfeaturebranch,we applyrandominitializationbecauseofthesmallnumberofparametersinvolvedandthesimple structure.Bothcharacteristicsmakethebrancheasiertotrain. 4.4.2Modelevaluation Aftertraining,themodelisevaluatedonthetestset.Weevaluatethemodelperformancewitha confusionmatrixthatshowstherecall,precision,andF1scoresofeachclass.Theconfusionmatrix presentsnotonlytheoverallperformancebutalsohowmisclassifiedsamplesarecategorized.Recall iscalculatedusingthenumberoftruepositives(TP)dividedbythesumoftruepositivesandthe numberoffalsenegatives(FN)4.1.Precisionisthenumberoftruepositivesoverthesumoftrue positivesandthenumberoffalsepositives4.2.F1-scoreistheharmonicmeanofprecisionand recall4.3. 4.4Method 73 TP (4.1) Recall= TP+FN TP (4.2) Precision= TP+FP Precision∗Recall (4.3) F1=2∗ Precision+Recall 4.4.3Modelablationstudy Besidesthethree-branchmodel,wealsopredicttheHtDhousesfromdifferentcombinationsof inputfeatures.Themainpurposeofthisablationstudyisto:1)exploretheminimaldataneeded forhigh-accuracypredictionofHtDhouses;2)understandthecontributionofeachbranchinthe three-branchmodel.Themodelarchitectureisthesameasourthree-branchmodelandcorresponds totheinputfeatures.Twogroupsofexperimentswiththreemodelsrespectivelyareconducted. Thefirstgroupofexperimentscontainsmodelswithonlyoneinputdata,namelySVI,AVIand tabulardata(LSTandbuildingarea).Thesecondgroupincludesmodelswithtwodatasources, whichareSVI+AVI,SVI+tabulardata,andAVI+tabulardata.Tomakesuretheresultsare comparableandavoidrandomnessduringexperiments,wealsokeepthesamesplitoftraining, validation,andtestdatasetacrossdifferentmodels. 4.4.4Modelinterpretation Thoughourmodelmakesclassificationinimage-levellabels,itisstillworthexploringwhatelements withintheimagesleadtothepredictionsothatfutureimprovementcouldbemade.Classactivation map(CAM)isusedtoidentifydiscriminativeregionsusedbyDCNNtomakepredictions(Zhou etal.,2016).Specifically,weaddaglobalaveragepoolinglayertothefinalconvolutionallayerof theoriginalmodel.Thentheweightedsumofoutputsfromtheglobalaveragepoolinglayeris calculatedasthefinaloutputs.BylookingattheCAM,weareabletounderstandwhichpartsof theimagesourmodelpaysthemostattentionto,andwhichleadtothefinalclassification.CAMis computedforbothSVIandAVIbranchesonthetestset. Table4.3reportstheconfusionmatrixforthethree-branchmodel.Theoverallaccuracyforthe modelis81.72%.Morespecifically,theprecisionsforHtDhousesandnon-HtDhousesare89.90% and71.43%.ThismeansthatifasampleispredictedbyourmodelasanHtDhouse,theprobability ofthehousebeinganHtDhouseis89%,whileifapropertyispredictedasanon-HtDhouse,the chanceofcorrectpredictionis71%.Besidesprecision,wealsoincluderecallandF1-scoreinthe confusionmatrix.Whileprecisionfocusesonhowthemodelmakesacorrectprediction,recallis abouttheabilitytofindallrelevantsamples.AsshowninTable4.3,ourmodelhasahigherrecall fornon-HtDhousesthanHtDhouses,withtheformerbeing0.84andthelatterbeing0.79. 4.5.2Modelablationstudy Withmulti-sourcedataandhigh-dimensionalfeatures,itisimportanttounderstandthecontri- butioneachdatasourcemakestotheprediction.Tobetterunderstandthis,amodelablation studyisperformedwithdifferentcombinationsofthedatasources.Theresultispresentedin 4.5Results 75 Table4.4ModelPerformanceComparison InputFeaturesPrecision[%]Recall[%]F1-Score[%] StreetViewImage(SVI)78.50%79.35%78.92% AerialViewImage(AVI)76.91%77.00%76.95% LandSurfaceTemperature(LST)+BuildingStock(BS)62.29%60.02%58.66% SVI+AVI79.43%79.37%79.40% SVI+LST+BS78.68%78.94%78.57% AVI+LST+BS79.53%79.18%79.31% SVI+AVI+LST+BS81.72%80.26%80.57% Table4.4.Column1ofthetableintroducesthecombinationofinputfeatures.Theremaining columnsillustratethemacro-averagedprecision,recall,andF1-Scoreofthemodels.Therowsin eachsub-sectionofthetablearesortedindescendingorderbyF1-score. Theablationstudyshowsthatgenerallymoredatasourcesresultinanincreaseintheprediction performance.Forthesingle-sourcegroup,theSVIaloneachievesthebestprecisionof78.50%.Both SVIandAVIaloneareabletopredictmorethan76%samplescorrectly.Themodeltrainedwith tabulardata,LSTandbuildingstockcombined,hasaprecisionof62.29%.Fortwo-sourcemodels, allmodelsachieveaprecisionofmorethan78%.ThemodeltrainedonSVIandAVIhasthebest recallandF1-score,whiletheAVIandLSTdatamodelachievesthebestprecisionof79.53%. 4.5.3Modelinterpretation Toexplorehowthemodelmakesdecisions,CAMisappliedtotestsamplesofthedatasetforboth SVIandAVImodels.Figure4.5showstheresultsforoverlayingCAMontoGSVimages.The figureindicatesclearpatternsofCAMresultswithsemanticobjectsbeinghighlightedratherthan randompartsofthebuildings.Forbuildingswhoseroofsareclearlyvisible,themodeltendstopay moreattentiontotheroofandchimney.Forthoseonlypartofthefacadesareshown,windowsare highlightedandattractthemostattention.Thepavement,carsinthefrontoftheimages,andtrees areignoredautomaticallyastheyareirrelevanttothetask.Similarpatternsalsohappeninsatellite images.Figure4.6showsthatformostofthesamples,themodelpaysattentiontothebuildings ratherthanroads,courtyards,andvegetation.What’smore,whenbuildingformisconsistent withintheimages,themodelpaysattentiontolargerareas(wherereddistributeswithintheimage). Verifyingtheeffectivenessoftheimagedataisbeneficialforthenet-zerotargetontheglobalscale. 4.6.2Limitationandfuturework Themainlimitationofourstudyliesinthepossibledatabias.Firstofall,thedefinitionofHtD housesisnotuniversallyagreedupon.Currently,wefollowtheguidanceofLocalGovernment AssociationUK.Thoughfarfromperfection,thiscriterionisthebestdefinitionwecouldfind. Also,thecurrentcriteriononlymeasuresthephysicalattributesofthehousessuchaswallstructure, roofform,andbuildingage.OtherconditionsofHtDhousesarenotincludedsuchaslocation, powersourceandoccupants’behaviours. Identification:Multi-ModalLearningfromBuildings 78 Futureworkwilltakeamorecomprehensiveconsiderationwithmorehousingattributesand occupants’behaviours.Moredatasourcessuchassmartmeterdatawillbehelpfulforthis. 4.7Conclusion Currentresearchondecarbonisingbuildingsfocusesmoreonpredictionandunderstanding buildingenergy-relatedaspectsforgenericbuildings.WiththeUK’saimofdecarbonisingthe residentialsectorby2050,ithasbecomeincreasinglyimportanttoaddressHtDhousingproblems. Inthisresearch,weproposeanapproachthatappliesdeeplearningandimagedataforthestudyof HtDhousingclassification. Inthiswork,weproposeaframeworkforidentifyingHtDhouses.AdatasetforHtDbuildings isorganizedforCambridge,UK.Wealsotrainadeeplearningmodelbasedonthedatasetand achieveanaccuracyof82%.Besidesprediction,anablationstudyisalsoconductedtoexplorehow differentdatasourcescontributetothefinalprediction.WefindthatSVIaloneisabletopredict theHtDhouseswithanaccuracyof78%.Withtheproposedframework,policymakersareableto understandtherealsituationandproposepoliciesforneighbourhoodorevenbuilding-levelnet- zerotargets.Thisstudyalsoimplementsananalysisofhowthemodelmakesthedecisionperformed withCAM.Resultsshowthatthemodelisabletofindmeaningfularchitecturalelementswithin theimage.
