sections.heading: UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss
sections.text: IntheUK,28millionhouseholdsconsume25%ofthetotalenergyand ChapterSummary. contributeto25%oftheemissions.Itisvitaltofocusonsustainabilityandenergyefficiencywithin thebuildingsectorfordecarbonisingpurposes.However,traditionalmethodsthatinvolvedetailed buildingsimulationoron-siteinspectionsarebothtime-consumingandlabour-intensive.Recent advancementsinhigh-resolutionthermalinfraredimages,streetviewimagedatasets,widelyavailable buildingattributes,anddeeplearningmethodologiesofferthepotentialtoestimatebuildingenergy efficiencyatalargescale.Inthisresearch,weproposeanovelmethodologyframeworkforclassifying energyefficiencyinbuildingsusingonlyexternalandwidelyavailablebuildingdata.Wehave designedandtrainedanend-to-endmulti-channeldeeplearningmodelutilisinghigh-resolution thermalinfraredandopticalsatelliteimages,streetviewimages,andbuildingmorphologicaldata. BuildingenergyperformancedatasetsfromGlasgowandEdinburghwereorganisedforthisstudy. Applyingthetrainedmodel,ourworkflowachievedF1scoresof0.64and0.69forthetwocities, respectively.Furtherpredictionsandanalysesrevealapositivecorrelationbetweenneighbourhood- levelbuildingenergyefficiencyandsocioeconomicdeprivation,suggestingthatmoredeprived neighbourhoodstendtohavehigherlevelsofbuildingenergyefficiency.Thisresearchdemonstrates howexternalbuildingdataanddeeplearningcanbeleveragedtoassessbuildingenergyefficiency andhasthepotentialtobeappliedgloballytoaddressthenet-zerocarbonagendaforsustainable livinginthefuture. UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 86 5.1Introduction Monitoringenergyconsumptioninbuildingsisofgreatimportanceforsustainableurbandevelop- mentandisacommongoalworldwide.Thebuildingsectoraccountsfor40%ofthetotalglobal primaryenergyconsumption,andmorethan50%ofbuildingenergyconsumptioncomesfrom energyoperation(Allouhietal.,2015;Buildings,2019;Yangetal.,2020).Scientificcontroland monitoringofenergyconsumptioninbuildingsisthereforeessentialforsustainableurbandevel- opment.Theimportanceofenergysustainabilityincitiesworldwideisrepeatedlyemphasizedin Goal7(Ensuresustainableenergysuppliesandpromoteuniversallyaccessible,reliable,sustainable, andmodernenergyservicesforall)andGoal11(Makecitiesandhumansettlementsinclusive, safe,resilient,andsustainable)oftheSustainableDevelopmentGoalsoftheUnitedNations.The EnergyPerformanceCertificate(EPC)wasintroducedin2002bytheEnergyPerformanceof BuildingsDirective(Directive2002/91/EC)asacompulsoryrequirementforEUmemberstates (MSs)inthecontextofconstructing,selling,orrentingabuildingordwelling(Lietal.,2019).Asa result,theUKimplementedtheEPCin2008torequirebuildingstoprovideanEPCwhenthey aresoldorrentedtoassessthebuilding’senergyefficiencyandCOemissions,andtoprovide 2 advicetoimprovethebuilding’senergyefficiencyandreduceitsenvironmentalimpact. Assessingtheenergyefficiencyofbuildingspresentsaformidablechallengeinformaland academiccontexts.Theconventionalapproachtoassessingtheenergyefficiencyofhousespredom- inantlyreliesonhumanaudits,necessitatingtheorganizationofregularvisitsandmeasurements byasubstantialnumberofstafftoevaluatetheenergyefficiencyofresidentialpropertieswithina givencity(Seyedzadehetal.,2018).Whilethismethodyieldsarelativelypreciseassessment,itis bothtime-consumingandlabour-intensive.AlthoughthepolicyforproducingEPChasexisted formorethan15years,therearestillaround50%ofbuildingsintheUKthatdonothaveavalid EPC.Otherstatisticalorbuildingsimulationmethodscanbeappliedtoestimatebuildingenergy efficiency,butthechallengealsoarisesfromtheintricateinterplaybetweenenergyconsumption andvariousfactors,suchastheaestheticsofthebuilding,thematerialsused,theinsulationlevel, andeventheageofthestructure.Consequently,classifyingabuilding’senergyefficiencysolely throughstatisticalorsimulation-basedmethodsbecomesincreasinglychallengingduetothesheer 5.1Introduction 87 Table5.1NomenclatureandAbbreviations AbbreviationNotationAbbreviationNotation EPCEnergyPerformanceCertificateSVIStreetViewImage AVIAerialViewImageTIRThermalInfrared SIMDScottishIndexofMultipleDeprivationGrad-CAMGradient-weightedClassActivationMapping SVMSupportVectorMachineNLLNegativeLog-Likelihood numberofbuildingsrequiringassessmentandthetime-intensivenatureoftraditionalevaluation processes. Toovercomethesechallenges,weproposeamodeltoevaluatebuildingenergyefficiencyef- ficientlywithonlyexternaldatafromtheindividualbuildings,basedonanend-to-enddeep learningarchitecture.Thisframeworkintegratesonlyexternaldataofbuildings,includingaerial high-resolutionthermalinfrared(TIR)images,streetviewimages(SVIs),aerialphotography,and buildingmorphologicalfeatures.Byharnessingthemulti-sourceinformation,ourmodelextracts pertinentfeaturesandsubsequentlyclassifiesthemintoHigh-performanceandLow-performance buildingsbasedonthedefinitionfromtheEnergyPerformanceCertificate.Weimplementand testtheframeworkintwomajorcitiesinScotland,includingGlasgowandEdinburgh,andpredict thebuildingenergyefficiencyforthosebuildingswithouthuman-generatedEPCs.Themodel’s abilitytoconductlarge-scaleevaluationsautomaticallysignificantlyenhancesoverallefficiencyin thiscontext. Themaincontributionofourresearchisasfollows:(1)Weproposeanovelmethodtofacilitate large-scaleautomatedenergyefficiencysurveys,providingvaluableinsightsintotheenergyperfor- manceofbuildings;(2)weproposeascalableframeworktoestimateandpredictbuildingenergy performancewithonlywidelyavailableexternaldatasets;(3)weanalysetherelationshipsbetween buildingenergyefficiencyanddeprivationlevel,whichhelpsusbetterunderstandtheareaneeding furtherbuildingretrofittingintwomajorScottishcities.Ultimately,suchknowledgeandanalysis frameworkcanbeappliedworldwideandcouldprovideeffectiverecommendationsforhousing retrofittingpolicyandconstructmoresustainablecities. UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 88 5.2Background 5.2.1Buildingenergyefficiencymodellingandpredictionthroughdeep learning Inrecentyears,numerousstudieshavebeendedicatedtoleveragingnewformsofdatatoevaluate andpredictbuildingenergyefficiency.Thesemethodscanmainlybedividedintothefollowing categories:engineeringcalculations,benchmarktestingbasedonsimulationmodels,statistical modelling,andmachinelearning/deeplearningapproaches(Foucquieretal.,2013;Fumo,2014). Engineeringcalculationsandsimulationmethodsrelyonphysicalprinciples,simulatingthether- malbehaviourofbuildingsbysolvingequationsthatdescribethephysicalbehaviourofheattransfer (ANSYS,2017).Thesemethodsaresuitableforbuildingsofvariousscalesandapplications.Al- thoughthismethodcanprovideaccurateresults,itrequiresdetailedknowledgeofthebuilding’s structure,thermalcharacteristics,materialproperties,andthesurroundingenvironment,which areoftendifficulttoobtain. Statisticalmethodsuseacomprehensivesetoffactors,includinginternalenergyconsumption factors,housingattributes,andenvironmentalfactors,toestablishregressionequations(Fumo andRafeBiswas,2015;TsoandGuan,2014)andestimatetheenergyconsumptionofahouse, suchaselectricityandgasconsumption(CiullaandD’Amico,2019;Minetal.,2010;Tsoand Guan,2014).Thismethodsimplifiesthedatarequirements,butitsestimatedresultsmostlycannot addressnonlinearproblemsforcomplexbuildings(Seyedzadehetal.,2018;).Machinelearning ? methods,suchasSupportVectorMachines(SVM),XGBoost,andrandomforest,predictenergy consumptionandenergyefficiencybyanalysinghousestructure,housearea,andmeteorological data(Seyedzadehetal.,2019).Thesemethodscanhandlecomplexnumericaldatasetsandprovide accuratepredictions(Fathietal.,2020). Furthermore,deeplearningmethodshavebecomeincreasinglypopularinthefieldofbuilding energy-relatedstudiesinrecentyears(Mayeretal.,2023a;Mohandesetal.,2019;Somuetal.,2021; Sunetal.,2022a).Theadvantageofthismethodisthatitcancombinemulti-sourceandnon- 5.2Background 89 traditionaldatasuchasbuildingexternalandinternalimagesthroughnonlinearmeanstoperform spatio-temporaldynamicassessmentsofbuildingenergyefficiency. 5.2.2Usingmulti-sourceimagedatatoevaluatebuildingenergyefficiency Toestimateandpredictbuildingenergyefficiency,manyresearchmethodsrelyonhouse-related internalinformation,suchasthespecificlocationofthehouse,yearofconstruction,layout,Heat- ing,Ventilation,andAirConditioning(HVAC)andelectricalapplianceconfigurations,andeven privateinformation,includingthenumberofresidentsandtheiroccupations(Mohandesetal., 2019).However,theneedtocollectsuchhigh-qualityinformationlimitstheimplementationof large-scalehouseholdenergyefficiencymodelling.Incontrast,thehousingexternalandinternal imagedatahasbecomeapowerfuldatasourceforhouseholdenergyefficiencyanalysisandpre- dictions,duetoitsmuchwideravailabilityandabilitytodescribeandcapturehousefeaturesin detail. Streetviewimages(SVI)fromcommercial(i.e.Google,Microsoft)oropen-source(i.e.Kartaview, Mapillary)providerscancapturedetailedexteriorinformationofhouses,suchaswindows,doors, andbuildingfacades.Usingdeeplearningtechnology,wecanextractkeyarchitecturalattribute informationfromtheexteriorappearance,suchastheyearofconstruction(Sunetal.,2022b), facadematerials,andtheratioofwindowstofacades,allofwhichareimportantfactorsinestimating theenergyefficiencyofahouse(Mayeretal.,2023b;SunandBardhan,2024;Sunetal.,2022a). Ontheotherhand,overheadimageseffectivelyrecordthematerialandshapeofhouseroofs, whichcanreflecttheageofthehouseandattributesrelatedtoenergyconsumption,suchas whetherphotovoltaicpanelshavebeeninstalledontheroof.Remotesensingimagesandaerial photographsaretwomainsourcesofoverheadimages.Althoughremotesensingimagesare updatedmorefrequently,theyhavelowerspatialresolution,aerialphotographsprovidemore detailedinformationabouturbanbuildings,suitableforhouseaudits.Inaddition,night-time remotesensingimagesshowtheintensityofurbanlightingatnight,whichiscloselyrelatedtothe city’selectricityconsumptionandisoftenusedtopredicturban-levelenergyconsumption. TIRimageryintuitivelyshowsthelevelsofheatleakagefromdifferenthouses,providingvaluable informationfordirectlyunderstandingtheenergyconsumptionofhouses.Giventhewideavail- UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 90 ability,lowcostofacquisition,andhighqualityofhouseinformationfromimagedata,integrating variousimagedatatopredicthouseenergyconsumptionisattractingincreasingattention(Mayer etal.,2023a,b). 5.3StudyAreaandData Ourstudyestablishesanewdatasettosupportourclassificationofhousingenergyperformance, whichincludeshigh-resolutionTIRimages,SVI,aerialimages,generalbuildingcharacteristics, andtheEPClabelofthehouseacrossGlasgowandEdinburgh,UnitedKingdom. 5.3.1StudyArea EdinburghandGlasgowwereselectedasthestudysitesduetotheirstatusasScotland’stwo largestcitiesandtheirwiderangeofbuildingagesandarchitecturalstyles,reflectingcenturiesof urbanevolution.Bothlocationsshareacool,temperateclimatewithrelativelyhighhumidityand frequentrainfall,buttheyalsoexhibitsubtlemicroclimaticvariationsinfluencedbygeography, coastalproximity,anddifferingurbanforms.Analysingbuildingsinthesecontrastingsettingsthus offersavaluableopportunitytocompareenergyperformanceacrossdiverseclimaticconditions, buildingtypologies,andurbandensities. 5.3.2Data Buildingfootprints Asoneofthemostfundamentaldatawithinthisstudy,thebuildingfootprintsareusedasthe referenceforlinkingdifferentdatasources.ThebuildingfootprintdataisobtainedfromtheUK 1Buildingsdataset.Thedatasetprovides2Dboundariesofbuildingfootprintsacrossthewhole UK. 1https://digimap.edina.ac.uk/. 5.3StudyAreaandData 91 EnergyPerformanceCertificates TheEPCdatacapturespreciseenergyperformancedetailsoftheproperties,includingthedi- mensionsandconfigurationoftheproperty,andtheapproachesforitsconstruction,insulation, heating,ventilation,andlighting.Withthisinformation,theEPCassessorsapplyacalculation techniquetoprojecttheproperties’energyconsumptiontoan“EnergyEfficiencyRating”.The ratingrangesfromAtoG,withArepresentingthehighestefficiencyandGrepresentingthelowest efficiency.InlightoftheUKgovernment’sgoaltoupgradeprivaterentedpropertiestoanEPC bandofCorhigherby2030,weclassifiedEPCratingsinto“HighEfficiency”(originalbandsAto C)and“LowEfficiency”(originalbandsDtoG). TheUKgovernmentprovidestheopen-accessEPCdata(Allouhietal.,2015;MHCLG,2020). TheEPCdataforGlasgowandEdinburghisavailablethroughtheScottishGovernment’sdata 2portal.Inthisstudy,weincorporatedtheEPCrecordsfromOctober2012toSeptember2023.We usedthezipcodeofGlasgowandEdinburghtoextractdatafromthewholeScottishEPCdatabase. TheaddressesofpropertiesfromEPCdatawerethengeocodedtoobtaintheaccuratelatitudeand 3longitudewithGoogleGeocodingAPI.ThegeocodedpropertieswithEPCinformationwere thenspatiallyjoinedwithbuildingfootprintstoperformbuilding-levelprediction.Forbuildings withmorethanonepropertyandvariousEPCratings,themostfrequentlyoccurringrating(mode) isadoptedasthegradeforthebuilding. AerialTIRimages TIRimageswereacquiredfromairplanesatnighttimeusingabroadbandandpush-frameTIR imager(MICROTABI640)withaspectralrangeof3.7-4.8microns.Twoflightcampaignswere conductedwithinGlasgowcitybetween20:01-21:13,7thMarch2023,andbetween21:14-22:04,and 20thApril2023inGMT+0respectively.Meanwhile,twogroundsensorsweredeployedatthe heightof1.5mabovegroundandonegroundsensorwasplacedonaresidentialbuildingrooftop withintheflightareatomeasuretemperatureandrelativehumidityduringtheflightmission.A flightcampaignwascarriedoutwithinEdinburghcityfrom23:34,26thto02:05,27thJuly2022 2https://statistics.gov.scot/data/domestic-energy-performance-certificates 3https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/geocoding/overview UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 92 inGMT+1.TIRimageswerecapturedatapproximately2438.4metresabovegroundlevelwith 50%imageoverlap.Imagepreprocessingincludesgeorectification,orthorectification,radiometric correction,bad-pixelcorrection,andemissivity-adjustedcalibration.Allofthepreprocessingwas conductedbythevendor.Asaresult,TIRimagesofrelativeemissivitythatcanindicatetheenergy radiatedfromthegroundsurfacein3.5mspatialresolutionwereobtainedforthisstudy. Streetviewimages SVIsserveasastreet-leveldigitalreflectionofconstructedenvironments,offeringacrucialtoolfor theexplorationandanalysisofarchitectureandurbanareas.SVIshaveprovenabletoaccurately describebuildingsinthecity.Inthisstudy,wecollectSVIsbasedontheorientationofthefaçade ofthebuildingtorepresenttheexteriorcharacteristicsofthebuildings(Sunetal.,2022b). SVIsarecollectedthroughtheGoogleStreetMapplatform(Anguelovetal.,2010).Google provideshigh-qualitypanoramicSVIsthatcoveralmosteverydrivingstreetinourstudyarea.As oneofthemostpopularSVIsourcesintheworld,GoogleSVIssupportaseriesofurbanresearch fromthegreeneryauditiontotheurbansocio-economicatmosphere(Houetal.,2024,2025;Li etal.,2015;Zhangetal.,2019,2022).WerequestGoogleSVIsforallindividualbuildingsforfurther analysis.Thedetailedprocessofrequestisillustratedin(Sunetal.,2022b). Aerialimages Inthisresearch,aerialimagesareemployedtocapturethesurroundingcontextofthebuilding 4structures.ThedataisdownloadedfromGetmappingPlc,Digimap.Wedownloadedtheaerial imagetilesthatoverlapwithinourstudyarea.Thenthetilesarecroppedaccordingtothebounding boxofeachbuilding. Socioeconomicindicators Twosourcesofsocioeconomicdataareinvolvedinthisresearch.Thefirstiscensusdata,usedas inputtothedeeplearningmodel.Socioeconomicindicatorsareintroducedasaproxyforthehu- manfactorsofbuildings’energyusagebehaviour.Allsocioeconomicdataisdownloadedthrough 4EDINASocietyDigimapService.ThedatacontainsSocialgrade,Highestlevelofqualifica- 5.4Methodology 93 tion,Populationdensity,Occupancyrating,Long-termhealthproblemordisability,Household composition,Economicactivity,Countryofbirth,Familieswithdependentchildren,Carorvan availabilitytohousehold,Agebysingle-yearandAccommodationtype.Thecensusattributesare fromthe2011census.ThesocioeconomicinformationgatheredisspecifictotheOutputArealevel. EachbuildingisallocatedtoitscorrespondingOutputArea,andthesocioeconomiccharacteristics arethenlinkedtothesebuildings. ThesecondsourceofsocioeconomicdataistheScottishIndexofMultipleDeprivation(SIMD). Itisusedinthispapertocorrelatewithourpredictionresults.SIMDisarelativemeasurementof deprivationinScotland.Ifaregionislabelledas’deprived,’itmayindicatenotonlylowincome amongresidentsbutalsoascarcityofresourcesoropportunities.TheSIMDassessesthelevelof deprivationwithinalocalityacrosssevendomains:income,employment,education,health,access 5toservices,crime,andhousing.SIMDdataisobtainedfromtheScottishGovernmentwebsite. Inthisstudy,rankingofdeprivationforeachdatazoneisusedtorepresentthedeprivationlevel, withonebeingthemostdeprivedandlargernumbersrelatedtolessdeprivation. 5.4Methodology Ourstudyproposesanend-to-endmulti-channeldeeplearningframeworktofusetheAerial TIRimages,SVIs,aerialimages,andbuildingcharacteristicsextractedfrombuildingmorphology toestimatethebuildingenergyefficiency.Modelevaluationmethodsandmodelinterpretation areproposedtoevaluatetheeffectivenessofourframework.Anablationtestisconductedto understandthecontributionsfromdifferentdatasources.WeusedtheexistingEPCdataasour validationdataset.Finally,themodelisappliedtobuildingswithnogroundtruthEPClabelto understandbuildingenergyperformanceinbothcitiesatalargescale. 5.4.1End-to-enddeeplearningmodeldesign Leveragingthedetailedbuildingandimagedatasets,wedevelopadeepconvolutionalneural networktailoredforenergyefficiencyclassification.Thismodelisdesignedtosimultaneously 4https://digimap.edina.ac.uk/ 5https://www.gov.scot/collections/scottish-index-of-multiple-deprivation-2020/ UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 94 extractandlearnfromthevisualcuesofTIRimages,façadeimagesfromGoogleSVIs,aerialimages aswellasthegeneralattributesofhousessuchasmorphological,structuralandsocioeconomic dataofproperties.Thearchitectureofournetwork,asshowninFigure,unfoldsinfourprimary ?? phases:input,featureextraction,featurefusion,andoutput.Itoperatesthroughfourconcurrent streamsduringtheinputandfeatureextractionphases:threeforimagesandonefordescriptive features.Theimagestreamsareidentical,andweprocessimagedatausingaSwinTransformer(Liu etal.,2021)frameworkasbackbonetoextracta1024-dimensionalfeaturevectorforsubsequent fusion.Meanwhile,thedescriptivefeaturestreamemploysafullyconnectednetworkwithfour hiddenlayers,generatinga256-dimensionalfeaturevector.Inthefeaturefusionphase,thesestream vectorsaremergedintoasingle3328-dimensionalfeaturevector.Thiscomprehensivefeaturevector isthenfedintoafullyconnectednetworkwithtwohiddenlayers,culminatinginclassifyingthe building’senergyefficiencyintospecificcategories. Weemploydistinctinitializationstrategiesfortheimageandtabularfeaturebranches.The imagebranchesareinitialisedwithweightspre-trainedontheImageNetdataset(Dengetal.,2009), awidelyuseddatasetforrecognisingcommonobjectssuchasvehicles,buildings,andstreetsigns. Thesepre-trainedweightsenablethemodeltounderstandandextractinformationaboutobjects andsceneswithintheimages,facilitatingfasternetworkconvergenceandreducingtrainingtime. Incontrast,thetabularfeaturebranchisinitialisedrandomlyduetoitssmallnumberofparameters andsimplestructure. Acustomisedlossfunctionisemployedintheend-to-enddeeplearningmodel.Ratherthan calculatingthelosssolelyforthefinaloutput,anegativelog-likelihood(NLL)lossiscomputedfor eachbranchofthemodel.Thelossesfromallindividualbranches,alongwiththefinaloutput, arethenaggregated.Thisapproachensuresthateachbranchcontributeseffectivelytotheoverall prediction. 5.4.2Modelevaluationandablationtest Thetrainedmodelisthenevaluatedwiththetestset.Theevaluationmetricsadoptedinourstudy aremicro-averagedprecision,recallandF1score.Microaveragingconsiderseachobservationas weightedequallyratherthaneachclass.Theentiredatasetistreatedasasingleaggregateresult, 5.4Methodology 95 Figure5.1Modelarchitectureforend-to-endmodel. UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 96 computingoneoverallmetricinsteadofaveragingmultipleclass-specificmetrics.Recallisdeter- minedbydividingthenumberoftruepositives(TP)bythesumoftruepositivesandfalsenegatives (FN),asoutlinedinEquation(1).Precisioniscalculatedbydividingthenumberoftruepositives bythesumoftruepositivesandfalsepositives,asdepictedinEquation(2).TheF1-scorerepresents theharmonicmeanofprecisionandrecall,aspresentedinEquation(3). TP (5.1) Recall= TP+FN TP (5.2) Precision= TP+FP Precision∗Recall (5.3) F1=2∗ Precision+Recall Insteadofonlyfocusingonacomprehensivemodel,wealsoanalysethemodeltounderstand howdifferentdatasourcescontributetotheprediction.Combinationsofdifferentdatasources areusedtotrainmultiplemodels.Threegroupsofsinglesource,doublesources,andmultiple sourcesmodelsaretrainedandevaluated.Toensureconsistentresultsandminimiserandomness duringexperiments,wemaintainthesamesplitoftraining,validation,andtestdatasetsof70%, 15%,and15%acrossallmodels. 5.4.3Modelinterpretationandprediction Althoughourmodelclassifiesbasedonimage-levellabels,itisvaluabletoinvestigatewhichelements withintheimagescontributetothepredictions,enablingfutureimprovements.WeadoptedGrad- CAM(Selvarajuetal.,2019)toidentifytheregionscontributingthemosttothefinalprediction. WeusetheimplementationofJacobandcontributors(2021).ExaminingtheGrad-CAMallows ustoidentifywhichareasoftheimagesourmodelfocuseson,ultimatelyleadingtothefinal classification.WecalculatetheGrad-CAMforboththeSVIsandaerialimagebranchesusingthe testset,thenanalysetheresultsforeachimagetouncoverpatternsinthedata. 5.5Results 97 Table5.2ConfusionmatrixforHtDclassification CityGlasgowEdinburgh HighEfficiencyLowEfficiencyHighEfficiencyLowEfficiency HighEfficiency0.190.560.810.44 LowEfficiency0.450.190.550.81 F1-Score0.640.69 Currently,EPCcoversabout50%ofUKdomesticbuildings(Sunetal.,2022a).Tovalidatethe effectivenessofourframeworkandapplytheclassificationmodel,wecreateadatasetbycombining allthebuildingscoveredbyourhigh-resolutionTIRimages,SVIsandaerialimagesbutmissing EPClabelsforbothGlasgowandEdinburgh.Theend-to-endclassificationmodelperforms predictionstoallthebuildingsinthedatasetandclassifythemintobuildingswithHighEfficiency andLowEfficiency. 5.5Results 5.5.1End-to-endmodelperformance ThedatasetsareorganisedforGlasgowandEdinburghseparately.Thesedatasetscontainbuildings withallattributesdescribedfromSection5.3.TheGlasgowdatasetcontains55,018samples,with 26,859beinghigh-efficiencybuildingsand28,159beinglow-efficiencybuildings.TheEdinburgh datasetincludes6,631high-efficiencybuildings,7,037low-efficiencybuildings,andatotalsizeof 13,668buildings.EachsamplecorrespondstoanindividualbuildingandincludesprocessedGSV data,aerialTIRimagery,aerialphotography,generalbuildingattributes,andtheassociatedenergy label.Thedatasetissplitintotrain,validation,andtestsetswiththeproportionof70%,15%,and 15%. Twomodelsaretrainedbasedonthetrainingsplitofthesetwodatasets.Themodelandentire computationareimplementedinPythonandPyTorchonanUbuntuplatform,utilisingtwo GeForceRTX2080TiGPUs.Thetrainedmodelsarethenevaluatedwiththeheld-outtestset usingmicro-averagedprecision,recall,andF1-Score. Table5.2presentstheconfusionmatrixfortheend-to-endmodels.TheoverallF1scoresforthe GlasgowandEdinburghmodelsare0.64and0.69,respectively.Specifically,theprecisionforHigh UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 98 Table5.3Ablationstudyofbinarymodel(Glasgow) DataPrecisionRecallF1Score Singlesource SVI0.710.510.59 TIR0.520.700.60 Aerial0.670.560.61 Doublesource SVI+Aerial0.730.580.65 SVI+TIR0.690.620.66 Aerial+TIR0.660.560.61 Multiplesource SVI+Aerial+TIR0.720.570.63 SVI+Aerial+TIR+Socio0.750.550.64 EfficiencyhousesinGlasgowis0.81.ThisimpliesthatifahouseispredictedasHighEfficiencyby ourmodel,thereisan81%chancethatittrulybelongstothiscategory.Whilebothmodelspresent similarF1scores,theyexhibitdifferentpatternsforthetwoclasses.TheGlasgowmodelachieves higherprecisionforHigh-Efficiencybuildings,whiletheEdinburghmodelhasbetterperformance forLow-Efficiencyhouses. 5.5.2Ablationtestresult Giventhemulti-sourcedataandhigh-dimensionalfeatures,itiscrucialtounderstandthecontri- butionofeachdatasourcetotheprediction.Toclarifythis,weconductedamodelablationstudy usingvariouscombinationsofdatasources.TheresultsaredisplayedinTable5.3and5.4.Thefirst columnintroducesthecombinationsofinputfeatures,whilethesubsequentcolumnspresentthe macro-averagedprecision,recall,andF1scoreforeachmodel. Theablationstudyrevealsthat,ingeneral,incorporatingmoredatasourcesenhancesprediction performance,withtwo-sourceandmulti-sourcemodelsdeliveringsimilarresults.Inthesingle- sourcegroup,theSVIsaloneachievedthelowestF1scoreof0.59forGlasgowandthehighestof 0.68forEdinburgh.Bothhigh-resolutionTIRimagesandaerialimagesindividuallyachieveF1 scoresabove0.6.Amongthetwo-sourcemodels,allmodelsattainaprecisionofover0.6,andmost achieveanF1scoreexceeding0.65. 5.5Results 99 Table5.4Ablationstudyofbinarymodel(Edinburgh) DataPrecisionRecallF1Score Singlesource SVI0.600.780.68 TIR0.520.910.67 Aerial0.590.680.63 Doublesource SVI+Aerial0.630.730.68 SVI+TIR0.620.670.64 Aerial+TIR0.590.710.64 Multiplesource SVI+Aerial+TIR0.630.700.66 SVI+Aerial+TIR+Socio0.610.810.69 5.5.3Modelinterpretationresults Toinvestigatehowthemodelmakesdecisions,Grad-CAMisappliedtotestsamplesforboththe SVIsandaerialimagemodels.Figure5.2illustratestheresultsofoverlayingGrad-CAMonSVIs. ThefigurerevealsclearpatternsintheGrad-CAMresults,withsemanticobjectshighlightedrather thanrandompartsofthebuildings.Forbuildingswhereroofsareclearlyvisible,themodeltends tofocusontheroofandchimney.Incaseswhereonlypartsofthefacadesarevisible,windows arehighlightedanddrawthemostattention.Pavements,carsintheforeground,andtreesare automaticallyignoredastheyareirrelevanttothetask.Similarpatternsemergeinaerialimages, withthemodelpayingattentiontotheroofinsteadofsurroundingcontext(Figure5.3). 5.5.4Modelpredictionresult Tobetterillustratetheframeworkandimplythepolicyintervention,weapplyourmodeltopredict energyperformanceforhouseswithoutEPClabelsinGlasgowandEdinburgh.Followingthe similarstepsillustratedinSection5.3,adatasetisorganisedforbothcities.Thepredictiondataset contains90,954and45,090buildingsforGlasgowandEdinburgh,respectively. Weapplyourtrainedmodelfromtwocitiesoverthepredictiondatasets.Thepredictionismade onthebuildinglevel.Forbothcities,themajorityofbuildingsarepredictedtobeLow-efficiency, UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 100 Figure5.2ClassActivationMapforSVIchannel Figure5.3ClassActivationMapforaerialimagechannel 5.5Results 101 correspondingtoEPClabelsDtoG.Specifically,50,735outof90,954buildingsarepredictedas low-efficiencyinGlasgow.ForEdinburgh,33,164outof45,090arepredictedasLowEfficiency. Whilepredictionsaremadeforthebuilding-instancelevel,policyintervention,andactions areusuallymadeonanadministrativeunitlevel.Tobetterinformthepolicyandunderstandthe patternacrossthecity,theresultsareaggregatedintodatazone(censusunitinScotland)levelto matchwithSIMD,thedeprivationindicator.Ourresultsarespatiallyjoinedtothedatazoneand aggregatedto4,819and2,488datazonesinGlasgowandEdinburgh.Foreachdatazone,abuilding energyperformanceindicatoriscalculatedbydividingthecountofHighEfficiencybuildingsby thetotalbuildingsinthepredictiondataset. Tobetterunderstandtherelationshipbetweenpredictedbuildingenergyperformanceandde- privation,correlationanalysisisperformedforSIMDandbuildingenergyperformanceindicators. WeusetherankofSIMDtorepresentthedeprivationstatusforeachdatazone.FortheSIMD ranking,1representsthemostdeprived,andalargernumbermeanslessdeprivation.APearson correlationiscalculatedforbothGlasgowandEdinburgh.Bothcorrelationsexhibitsignificant relationships.Thecorrelationcoefficientsare-0.52and-0.35forGlasgowandEdinburgh.Itshows thatneighbourhooddeprivationandbuildingenergyefficiencyarepositivelycorrelated,meaning moredeprivedareashavebetterbuildingenergyperformance.Figure5.4illustratestherelation- shipsbetweenneighbourhoods’SIMDandenergyperformance.Inthismap,SIMDdeciles1-3are classifiedasareasoflowdeprivation,4-7asareasofmoderatedeprivation,and8-10asareasofhigh deprivation.Intermsofenergyperformance,theprimaryEPCratingsofbuildingswithinaregion serveastheevaluationcriterion,withratingsA-Ccategorizedashighenergyperformanceareas andD-Gaslowenergyperformanceareas.Areaswherehighdeprivationcoincideswithlowenergy performancearehighlightedindarkredinthevisualization,emphasizingthespatialdistribution ofthisdualdisadvantage. UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 102 Figure5.4Bivariatefactormapaboutrelationshipsbetweenneighbourhoods’deprivation(SIMD) andenergyperformance.(a)Edinburgh;(b)Glasgow.Areaswithhighdeprivation (SIMDdeciles8–10)andlowenergyperformance(EPCratingsD–G)arehighlightedin darkred,indicatingzonesofdualdisadvantage. 5.6Discussion 103 5.6Discussion 5.6.1Predictingbuildingenergyperformancewithonlyexternalbuilding datasets Withbuildingsbeingthelargestenergyconsumptionsector,improvingtheirefficiencyhelpsreduce greenhousegasemissionsandsupportsclimatechangemitigationandcarbon-neutralpolicies. TheUKaimstoupgradeprivaterentedpropertiestoEPCbandCorhigherby2030.Property ownersbenefitfromunderstandingcurrentratingsandtheimpactandcostofrenovations,while policymakerscanidentifypropertiesneedingimprovementforcompliance. FacingthechallengeofestimatingthewholebuildingstockintheUK,ourstudyusesonly externaldatatoclassifyandpredictthebuildingenergyperformancewithoutdetailedinspections ofhouses.Combiningtheexternaldataanddeeplearning,ourclassificationcanachievetheF1- scoreof0.64and0.69forGlasgowandEdinburgh.Furtherablationstudydiscussedhoweachdata sourcecontributestotheprediction.Theablationstudydemonstratesthat,overall,incorporating additionaldatasourcesimprovespredictionperformance.Withinthesingle-sourcecategory,the SVIsaloneachievetheF1scoresof0.59forGlasgowand0.68forEdinburgh.Bothhigh-resolution TIRimagesandaerialimagesindividuallyattainF1scoresabove0.6.Forthetwo-sourcemodels, allconfigurationsachieveaprecisiongreaterthan0.6,andmostexceedanF1scoreof0.65. Byfocusingonexternaldatatopredictbuildingenergyperformance,ourresearchharnessesthe powerofwidelyavailable,cost-effective,andcomprehensivedatasources.Thisapproachnotonly enhancesthescalabilityandconsistencyofourpredictivemodelsbutalsoensuresadaptabilityand improvedpredictionaccuracyacrossvariousbuildingtypesandlocations.Itdemonstratesthat SVIs,aerialimages,andhigh-resolutionTIRimagescanprovidevaluableinformation,notonly aboutvisibleattributeslikebuildingstylebutalsoaboutintrinsiccharacteristicssuchasenergy efficiency.Bycombiningmulti-sourceimagedatawithbuildingcharacteristicsandsocioeconomic factors,thestudyachievesrelativelyhighperformanceinestimatingproperty-levelenergyefficiency. UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 104 5.6.2Co-occurrenceofdeprivationandEnergyInefficienthouses Manypreviousliteraturelinkssocialdisadvantagegroupswithenergy-inefficienthouses.However, ourresults(asshowninSection5.5.4)showthatdeprivationlevelispositivelycorrelatedwith highenergyefficiencybuildings,atleastinourstudyarea.Thiscontradictsourexpectationthat wealthierneighbourhoodswouldhavebetterbuildingenergyperformance.Furtheranalysisis performedtoexaminethevalidityofourobservation.InFigure5.5weshowbothgroundtruth andpredictionofenergyperformanceintheneighborhoodlevelbySIMDdecilesforbothcities. Itrevealsthatforbothcities,groundtruth,andpredictionresultshavesimilardistributions,with mostdeprivedneighbourhoodshavingthehighestenergyperformance. Recently,Buyuklievaetal.(2024)reachedasimilarconclusionwhilestudyingthevariationsof theEPCdata.Buyuklievaetal.(2024)findlittleevidencetosuggestthatmoreaffluentgroupsare preferentiallychoosingmoreefficientpropertiesortakingactionathigherrates—quitethecontrary. Thismightbebecausethedeprivationneighbourhoodshavebeenextensivelytargetedforenergy efficiencyimprovementpoliciesorprogrammes.Alsoinaffluentarea,thecostofenergyisnotas sensitiveasthedeprivedhouseholds,andsometimespeoplelivinginaffluentareaswishtomaintain theirspecialbuildingarchitecturalstyleswithoutputtingextraexternalbuildinginsulation. 5.6.3Limitation Therearelimitationsinthisstudythatcouldbeaddressedinfutureresearch.Oneofthelimitations isthedataqualityoftheEPCdataset.OurworkreliesheavilyontheEPCdatasetanditiswell- documentedthatthereareuncertaintiesintheEPCdataset,particularlyregardingthegapbetween estimatedandactualenergyperformance(CoyneandDenny,2021;TronchinandFabbri,2012). However,withtheincreasingcoverageofEPC,Europeancountrieshaveestablishedstandards forqualityassurance.Futureworkcanfocusongraduallyminimisingtheseuncertaintiesinthe dataset. Anotherlimitationisthelimitedavailabilityofhigh-resolutionTIRimages,whichrestrictsthe generalizabilityandtransferabilityofourmethodtoothercontexts.Currently,thescarcityofthis dataposesachallengeforbroaderapplications.However,theincreasingnumberofhigh-resolution 5.7Conclusion 105 (a)(b) (c)(d) Figure5.5BoxplotsofneighborhoodlevelenergyperformanceandSIMD.Upperleft:Glasgow Prediction;LowerLeft:GlasgowGroundTruth;Upperright:EdinburghPrediction; LowerRight:EdinburghGroundTruth.Bothcitiesshowsimilardistributions,with themostdeprivedneighbourhoodsexhibitingthehighestenergyperformance. satelliteswiththermalinfraredsensorsbeinglaunchedisexpectedtoenhancedataavailabilityin thenearfuture.Asmorehigh-resolutionTIRimagesbecomeaccessible,weanticipatethatmore futureworkinotherareasoftheworldwillverifytheapplicabilityandrobustnessofourmethod, enablingitsuseinawiderrangeofcontextsandlocations. 5.7Conclusion Improvingbuildingenergyefficiencyisessentialforreducingglobalcarbonemissions.Accurately predictingandunderstandingenergyefficiencyinbuildingscanleadtobetterhousingretrofitting decisions,andhelpthetransitiontonet-zerocarbonforthebuildingsectors.Inthisresearch,we proposeanefficientandaccuratemodelforevaluatingbuildingenergyefficiencyusinganend- to-enddeeplearningarchitecture.Thisframeworkexclusivelyintegratesexternaldataofhouses, includingTIRimages,SVIs,aerialimages,andgeneralbuildingandhouseholdrelatedfeatures. Byleveragingthismulti-sourceinformation,ourmodelextractsrelevantfeaturesandclassifies buildingsintoHighenergyperformanceandLowenergyperformancecategoriesbasedonEPC bands.WeimplementedandtestedtheworkflowinGlasgowandEdinburgh,UK.Theexperimental UnderstandingtheLandscapeofHeatLoss 106 resultsshowthatourmodelachievesanF1-scoreof0.64forGlasgowand0.69forEdinburgh.Our researchcontributestothefieldbyintroducingamethodforconductinglarge-scale,automated energyefficiencysurveys,offeringvaluableinsightsintobuildingenergyperformanceestimation andprediction.Ourscalableframeworkisabletopredictbuildingenergyperformanceusingonly externaldata,withtheflexibilitytousedatasourcesindividuallyorincombination.Wefurther applythemodeltobuildingswithoutEPCenergyratingsinbothcitiesandexploretherelationship betweenenergyperformanceanddeprivation.Ourfindingsindicateapositivecorrelationbetween deprivationlevelsandhighenergyefficiencybuildings.Thiscontradictsourinitialexpectationsthat affluentneighbourhoodswouldexhibitbetterbuildingenergyperformance,andthepromotion ofenergyefficiencyimprovementfortheaffluentneighbourhoodsisnecessary.Thisresearch illustrateshowexternaldataanddeeplearningcanbeutilisedtoevaluatebuildingenergyefficiency. Thefine-grainedlarge-scalepredictionalsoenablesthepolicymakerstogainaclearunderstandingof thecurrentsituationanddeveloppoliciesaimedatachievingnet-zerotargetsattheneighborhood orevenbuildinglevel.

UnderstandingHeatStressinDomesticEnvironments 114 6.2Background 6.2.1DefinitionofHeatwaveandmini-heatwave Theclassificationofheatwavesremainsinconsistentandlacksauniversallyaccepteddefinition, posingchallengesforresearchersandpolicymakers(Zuoetal.,2015).Whilevariousmeteorological organizationsandstudieshaveproposedcriteria—suchastemperaturethresholds(Maetal.,2015), duration(Maetal.,2015;Xuetal.,2016),andregionalvariability(AndersonandBell,2009)—there isnostandardisedframeworkthatappliesgloballyorevennationally. IntheUK,aheatwaveisofficiallydeclaredwhenalocationrecordsatleastthreeconsecutivedays withdailymaximumtemperaturesreachingorsurpassingaspecificheatwavethreshold(McCarthy etal.,2019).Thisthresholdisnotstandardisednationwidebutinsteadvariesbycounty,accounting forregionalclimaticvariations.ForLondon,thethresholdissetat28degreesCelsius,reflecting thecity’suniqueclimaticconditionsandurbanheatcharacteristics. Theexistingdefinitionfocusessolelyonoutdoortemperatures,whichdonotalwaysaccurately reflectconditionswithindomesticenvironments.Asheatwavesbecomemorefrequentandintense duetoclimatechange,understandinghowtheseextremeeventsaffectindoorspacesiscritical. Althoughresearchonindooroverheatinghasexpandedrapidly(Pathanetal.,2017;Taylor etal.,2014),thefieldstilllacksacommonbenchmarkforwhatconstitutesan“overheat”event (Laouadietal.,2020).Withoutatleastanationallyorregionallystandardiseddefinitionofindoor heatwaves,itremainsimpossibletocomparestudies,identifyat-riskdwellings,orcrafttargeted policy.Establishingsuchadefinitionisthereforeacriticalnextstepforoverheatingresearchand practice. Weintroducetheconceptofanindoor“mini-heatwave”—ananaloguetotheUK’soutdoor heat-wavedefinitionbuttailoredtointeriorconditions.Unliketheoutdoorcriterion,which requiresthresholdtemperaturestopersistforthreeconsecutivedays,anindoormini-heatwavemay betriggeredoverashorterduration.Anadvantageofourmethodisitsflexibility—itallowsthe durationthresholdtobeadjustedbasedonspecificresearchobjectivesordataresolution,making itadaptabletodifferentcontextsorcomfortstandards.Weinitiallysettheminimumduration 6.2Background 115 thresholdto10minutesandconductedasensitivityanalysistoexaminehowvaryingthisparameter influencesthedetectionresults. Thresholdsshouldbesetnationallyorregionally.ForEngland,theHeatwavePlan(2011)already flags26°Castheupperlimitforsafeindoorconditions(Andersonetal.,2013;Council,2012). Thiscut-offisreinforcedbyevidencethatmortalityrisesonceindoortemperaturesexceed26°C (Klenketal.,2010).Accordingly,weadopt26°Castheworkingthresholdfordefininganindoor mini-heatwaveinthisstudy. 6.2.2Heatwaveanditsinfluenceondomesticenvironments Heatwaveshaveprofoundimplicationsfordomesticenvironments,affectinghumanhealth(McMichael etal.,2006),energyconsumption(Haddadetal.,2024),andsocio-economicequity(Chowetal., 2012).Researchhighlightsthatelevatedoutdoorandindoortemperaturessignificantlyimpair humanhealthandproductivity,andincreasecoolingenergydemand,leadingtohigherpeakelec- tricityloads(Santamouris,2020)andheat-relatedmortality(Goslingetal.,2009;Williamsetal., 2012).Deviationsfromoptimalindoortemperaturesdisrupthumanthermoregulation,causing discomfort,reducedproductivity,anddissatisfaction(Felgueirasetal.,2023;Yennetietal.,2020). Prolongedexposuretoextremeheatexacerbatescardiovascular,respiratory,andthermoregulatory issues,suchasstrokesandexhaustion,whilealsocontributingtocognitiveandmentalhealth disorders(Limaetal.,2020;Thametal.,2020).Theseeffectsareparticularlypronouncedinvulner- ablepopulations,includingtheelderly,thosewithpre-existinghealthconditions,andindividuals lackingfinancialorpublicsupport(Chowetal.,2012). Despitethesignificantimplicationsofheatwavesandindooroverheatingforhumanhealth, energyconsumption,andsocialequity,thedrivingfactorsbehindthesephenomenaandtheir relationshipwithoutdoorweatherconditionshavenotreceivedsufficientattentionintheliterature. Whilethepredictionofindoorbuildingtemperaturehasbeenextensivelystudied(e.g.,(Fangetal., 2016;LuandViljanen,2009;Ruanoetal.,2006;Soleimani-Mohsenietal.,2006)),thereremain limitationsinunderstandingtheunderlyinglinkbetweenoutdoorweatherpatternsandindoor thermalconditions.Thishighlightstheneedforfurtherresearchtoexplorethecomplexinterplay UnderstandingHeatStressinDomesticEnvironments 116 betweenexternalclimaticfactorsandindooroverheating,particularlyinthecontextofrisingglobal temperaturesandincreasingfrequencyofextremeheatevents. 6.3StudyAreaandData 6.3.1StudyArea ThestudyareaselectedforthisresearchisSouthwark,London.Londonhasatemperateoceanic climate(Cfb),classifiedaccordingtotheKöppenclimateclassification.Southwarkisaborough ofparticularsignificanceduetoitsuniquecharacteristicsandchallenges.In2021,itwasrecorded asoneofthehottestlocationsintheUK,makingitacriticalareaforstudyingheat-relatedissues. TheIMDshowsapositivecorrelationwithindoortemperature,indicatingthathouseslocated inlessdeprivedareastendtohavehigherindoortemperatures.Thisfindingisconsistentwiththe earlierconclusionthatbetter-maintainedhouseconditionsareassociatedwithincreasedindoor UnderstandingHeatStressinDomesticEnvironments 132 Table6.4Regressionresults-Independentvariablesandcoefficients Model1Model2Model3Model4Model5 Variable Outdoortemperature0.220.220.220.220.16 LaggedOutdoortemperature(6h)----0.13 Housecondition&socioeconomic Self-reporthousecondition0.180.100.400.390.42 EPCrating0.070.030.170.150.19 IMD0.430.650.130.190.01 Tenure Rentingfromalocalauthority/council--0.590.610.82 Rentingfromahousingassocia- tion/housingco-operativeorcharitable---0.49-0.47-0.44 trust Rentingfromaprivatelandlord---1.30-1.27-1.36 Sharedownership---0.79-0.73-0.76 HouseType Flat/apartment(high-rise,onamidto --0.47--0.06-0.03 highfloor) Terracedhouse-0.24-0.160.07 Constant18.3118.4618.8318.2616.93 0.210.230.280.290.332 R Referencecategories—Tenure:Homeownerwithmortgage;HouseType:Flat/apartment(high- Note: riseonalowfloor,orlow-rise). temperatures,suggestingapotentiallinkbetweensocio-economicstatus,housingquality,and thermalcomfort. 6.6Discussion 6.6.1Thecurseofgoodcondition Thefindingsofthisstudyrevealaparadoxicalrelationshipbetweenbuildingcondition,socio- economicstatus,andindoortemperatureduringthesummermonths.Buildingsingoodcondition andthoselocatedinlessdeprivedareas(asmeasuredbytheIndexofMultipleDeprivation,IMD) tendtoexhibithigherindoortemperatures.Althoughbetter-maintainedbuildingsandhighersocio- economicstatusaretypicallyassociatedwithimprovedlivingstandards,theymayinadvertently 6.6Discussion 133 contributetooverheatingduringsummer,raisingconcernsaboutthermalcomfort,healthrisks, andenergyconsumption. This"curseofgoodcondition"highlightsacriticalflawincurrentbuildingdesignandenergy efficiencystandards,whichoftenprioritisewinterperformanceattheexpenseofsummercomfort. Asglobaltemperaturesriseandheatwavesbecomemorefrequent,theunintendedconsequences ofwell-maintainedbuildingsmustbeaddressedtoensureyear-roundthermalcomfort.Thissocio- economicparadoxhighlightstheneedforamorenuancedunderstandingofthermalcomfortand energyefficiency.Whilebetterhousingconditionsandresourcesaregenerallydesirable,theymust bebalancedwithstrategiestomitigateoverheatinganditsassociatedrisks. Thefindingsofthisstudyhaveimportantimplicationsforpolicymakers,architects,andurban planners.Buildingcodesandenergyefficiencystandardsshouldberevisedtoaddressbothheating andcoolingneeds.Thisincludespromotingpassivecoolingstrategies,suchasnaturalventilation, shading,andreflectivematerials,toreduceindoorheatbuildup.Raisingawarenessabouttherisks ofindooroverheatingandpromotingadaptivebehaviours,suchasproperventilationandshading, canhelphouseholdsmanageindoortemperaturesmoreeffectively. 6.6.2Theinvisiblemini-heatwavesindomesticenvironments Thefindingsofthisstudycomplementcurrentheatwaveclassificationsystems,whichpredomi- nantlyfocusonoutdoormeteorologicalconditions.Theresearchrevealstheimpactofextreme heatonindoorenvironments.Furthermore,withvariationsinbuildingcharacteristicsandsocio- economicfactors,theimpactpresentsdistinctdifferences.Throughthisanalysis,itbecomesevident thatmany"mini-heatwaves"areoccurringwithindomesticenvironments,yetthisphenomenon remainslargelyunaddressedinbothresearchandpolicy. Ouranalysisshowsthatindoortemperaturescandiffersignificantlyfromoutdoortemperatures duetofactorssuchasbuildinginsulation,ventilation,andoccupantbehaviour.Asaresult,a heatwavedefinedbyoutdoormetricsmaynotaccuratelyreflectthethermalconditionsexperienced indoors.Prolongedexposuretomoderatelyhighindoortemperatures,evenifnotclassifiedasa heatwaveoutdoors,canhavesignificanthealthimpacts.These"mini-heatwaves"withinhomesare oftenoverlookeddespitetheirpotentialtoexacerbateheat-relatedillnesses. UnderstandingHeatStressinDomesticEnvironments 134 Thisstudyrevealsthatmanydomesticenvironmentsexperiencefrequentandprolongedperiods ofelevatedindoortemperatures,whichcanbecharacterisedas"mini-heatwaves."Theseepisodes areinfluencedbybuildingcharacteristicsandsocio-economicfactors. Thefindingsofthisstudyhighlighttheneedforaparadigmshiftinhowheatwavesaredefined andaddressed.Developingmetricsthataccountforindoorthermalconditions,suchasindoor temperaturethresholdsandduration,couldprovideamoreaccurateassessmentofheat-relatedrisks. Publichealthcampaignsandheatwavepreparednessplansshouldprioritisevulnerablepopulations andprovideresourcesforimprovingindoorthermalcomfort,suchassubsidisedcoolingsystems orheatreliefprogrammes. 6.6.3Limitation Whilethisstudyprovidesvaluableinsights,itisnotwithoutlimitations.First,therelianceon self-reporteddataforhouseconditionmayintroducebias.Futureresearchcouldincorporate objectivemeasuresofbuildingquality,suchasthermalimaging.Second,thestudyfocuseson indoortemperatureduringsummer;futureworkcouldexploreseasonalvariationsandtheirimpact onoccupanthealthandwell-being.Finally,theanalysisislimitedtoaspecificgeographiccontext; expandingthestudytootherregionswithdifferentclimaticandsocio-economicconditionswould enhancethegeneralizabilityofthefindings. 6.7Conclusion Therapidaccelerationofclimatechangeisdrivingasteadyincreaseinglobaltemperaturesanda riseinthefrequencyandintensityofextremeweatherevents.Thispresentsanurgentandgrowing challengeforcountriesliketheUK,whichhavetraditionallyenjoyedmildersummerclimates.This studyleveragesreal-worldsensordatatoanalyseindoortemperaturesandthermalcomfortlevels inLondonhomes,aswellastheirresponsetoaheatwaveduringoneoftheUK’shottestsummers onrecord.Usingthesensor-collecteddata,thefindingsrevealthatindoorthermalcomfortlevels frequentlyfalloutsidetheacceptablerangeinmostofthemonitoredhomes.Whilethehouses generallymitigatedtheimpactoftheoutdoorheatwavetosomedegree,briefbutintenseheat 6.7Conclusion 135 spikeswereobservedinthemajorityofcases.Additionally,thestudyhighlightsastrongcorrelation betweenindoortemperaturesandsocio-economicfactors,alongsidehousingconditions.By utilisingsensor-baseddata,thisresearchprovidesvaluableinsightsintohowresidentialbuildings respondtoextremeheatevents,offeringafoundationforfuturestrategiestoimprovethermal resilience.

sections.heading: Conclusion
sections.text: 180 8.2ContributionsoftheThesis Thisdissertationmakescontributionsatfourinterrelatedlevels:conceptual,methodological, empirical,andpolicy-relevant. 8.2.1ConceptualContributions Theprimaryconceptualcontributionofthisdissertationistheintroductionandoperationalisation ofasananalyticalframeworkforunderstandingyear-roundthermalvul- doublethermaljeopardy nerabilityindomesticenvironments.Priortothiswork,wintercoldstressandsummeroverheating werelargelytreatedasparallelbutseparateconcerns,eachwithitsownmetrics,policyinstruments, andresearchtraditions.Thisdissertationdemonstratesthattheyarestructurallylinked,arising fromthesamecombinationofhard-to-retrofitbuildingfabric,socio-economicconstraint,and limitedadaptivecapacity.Thedoublethermaljeopardyframingreorientsanalyticalattentionaway fromseason-specifichazardstowardamoreintegratedunderstandingofcompoundvulnerability embeddedinhousingsystems. Relatedtothis,thedissertationdevelopsandappliestheconceptof:house- thermalminorities holdswhoseeverydaythermalexperiencessystematicallyfalloutsidenormativeassumptionsof comfortandsafety.Bygroundingthisconceptinempiricaldataspanningidentification,heat-loss mapping,indoormonitoring,andelectricityrhythms,theresearchmovesthenotionofthermalin- equalitybeyondabstracttheorisationtowardapracticallymeasurableandpolicy-relevantcategory. Thiscontributionextendsestablishedenvironmentaljusticeanddoublejeopardyscholarship— previouslyappliedinpublichealth,racialinequality,andneighbourhooddisadvantageresearch— intothedomainofdomesticthermalenvironments. 8.2.2MethodologicalContributions Thedissertationmakesfourprincipalmethodologicalcontributions.First,itestablishesascalable multi-modaldeeplearningframeworkforidentifyingHtDbuildingsfrompubliclyavailable observationaldata.Bydemonstratingthat82%classificationaccuracyisachievablewithopen imagedataalone,andthatsingle-modalityapproaches(SVI:78%)remainhighlycompetitive,the 8.2ContributionsoftheThesis 181 researchprovidesapracticallydeployablemethodologythatcanbeextendedtobuildingstocks beyondtheUK,includingdata-scarcenationalcontexts. Second,thedissertationintroducesandvalidatesamethodologyforlarge-scaleenergyefficiency classificationusingexternalbuildingdata,extendingassessmentstothesubstantialproportion ofbuildingswithoutformalEPCrecords.Theintegrationofthermalinfraredsatelliteimagery, streetviewimages,aerialphotography,andmorphologicaldescriptorswithinasingleSwinTrans- formerarchitectureprovidesaflexibleandtransferablepipelinefornational-scalebuildingenergy diagnostics. Third,thedissertationintroducestheconceptandacorresponding indoormini-heatwave event-detectionalgorithmgroundedinhigh-resolutionindoorsensordata.Bydefiningindoor overheatingasatemporallypersistentphenomenon,independentofoutdoormeteorological classifications,thisapproachenablesdirectcomparisonbetweenindoorandoutdoorthermal eventsandcapturesthelagdynamicsofheataccumulationwithinbuildings.Thismethodological contributionaddressesarecognisedgapinoverheatingresearch,wheretheabsenceofstandardised indooreventdefinitionshasimpededcross-studycomparisonandpolicydevelopment. Fourth,thetwo-stepActivity-BasedHouseholdType(AHT)classificationframeworkoffersan interpretablemethodologyforcharacterisingdomesticelectricityusestylesatscale.Byseparating consumptionvolumefromtemporalrhythm,andgroundingclassificationsinbuildingcharac- teristicsandsocio-economiccontextthroughexplainablemachinelearning,theAHTframework movesbeyondaggregateenergymetricstowardaricher,behaviour-sensitiverepresentationof residentialelectricitydemand. 8.2.3EmpiricalContributions Empirically,thedissertationcontributesnewevidenceoneachdimensionofdoublethermaljeop- ardy.InthedomainofHtDidentification,itprovidesthefirstlarge-scale,image-basedclassification ofHtDbuildingsinaUKcontext,demonstratingboththefeasibilityoftheapproachandthe discriminativevalueofindividualdatasources.Inthedomainofheat-lossmapping,itproduces city-wideenergyefficiencyestimatesforGlasgowandEdinburgh,coveringapproximately136,000 buildingsacrossbothcitiesandrevealingthespatialco-occurrenceofdeprivationandinefficiency.
